proposals.title: Quantifying and Mitigating Contextual Reward Drift in Scalable Educational Feedback Interventions
proposals.motivation: Educational technology increasingly deploys automated interventions (e.g., feedback prompts, practice suggestions) proven effective in specific, often small-scale, studies. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in understanding how the underlying 'reward function'—the model of what constitutes effective learning—degrades or 'hacks' when applied to new, diverse student populations and course contexts. This matters because scaling ineffective or harmful interventions wastes resources and can exacerbate educational inequities.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance of a feedback intervention policy, when deployed at scale, will exhibit significant 'contextual reward drift'—a measurable divergence between its intended reward function and its actual impact—that correlates with specific, measurable features of the new educational context.
proposals.method: We will frame this as a large-scale offline policy evaluation and reward repair problem. First, we will collect a multi-institution dataset of student interactions (e.g., from MOOCs or LMS platforms) where a target intervention (e.g., a specific feedback template from an LLM-augmented system like Bloom) was deployed. Using offline contextual bandit methods (aligned with Brunskill's prior work), we will estimate the policy's heterogeneous treatment effects across contexts. We will then train a meta-model to predict performance degradation from context features (e.g., prior knowledge distribution, course topic) and use techniques inspired by 'Repairing Reward Functions with Feedback' to propose context-specific reward function corrections, validated via counterfactual simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formal framework and metrics for 'contextual reward drift' in educational AI, 2) a data-driven methodology for auditing intervention scalability before wide deployment, and 3) practical algorithms for context-aware policy adaptation. It directly advances the field of scalable, equitable educational technology by moving beyond single-context validation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work customizes interventions *within* a context using bandits, this proactively diagnoses and mitigates failure modes when transferring a *policy itself* across vastly different contexts, linking reward hacking concepts from RL safety to real-world educational scaling challenges.

proposals.title: Inverting the Nudge: A Causal Offline Bandit Framework to Test the Limits of Behavioral Interventions for Student Persistence
proposals.motivation: Online learning platforms face a critical challenge with student dropout. Pre-2026 work often assumes low-cost behavioral nudges (e.g., reminder emails, progress notifications) are a sufficient primary tool to improve persistence. This assumption may be flawed, leading to suboptimal resource allocation and masking deeper structural issues in course design or student support. A rigorous test of this core assumption is needed to guide effective intervention policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Behavioral nudges alone are insufficient to causally improve long-term persistence for a significant subset of struggling online students; their effect is bounded and contingent on unaddressed contextual factors like prior knowledge or self-efficacy.
proposals.method: We will use high-dimensional offline data (log traces, performance, demographics) from a MOOC platform to train a contextual bandit model that predicts the causal effect of a nudge (treatment) vs. no-nudge (control) on persistence. Drawing from pre-2026 offline policy evaluation and 'Repairing Reward Functions' methods, we will define a reward function balancing persistence and engagement metrics, then use doubly robust estimation to evaluate the historical nudge policy. We will then use the model to identify student context clusters where the estimated treatment effect is near-zero, providing evidence for the insufficiency hypothesis. An LLM-augmented analysis (inspired by 'Bloom') will qualitatively interpret these clusters from student forum text.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a causal, data-driven framework to audit the limits of lightweight interventions in education. It will contribute a novel offline evaluation method for educational policies and offer concrete evidence to shift the field's focus from optimizing nudges to diagnosing when and for whom more substantive support is required.
proposals.novelty: It directly inverts the dominant 'nudge-first' paradigm in educational technology by testing its sufficiency, rather than its optimization. It combines offline causal inference from bandits with LLM-based interpretability to move beyond average treatment effects to understand failure modes.

proposals.title: Adaptive Educational Interventions via Contextual Bandits with LLM-Reward Repair
proposals.motivation: Educational interventions often fail due to contextual heterogeneity across students and courses, leading to one-size-fits-all policies that are suboptimal. Pre-2026 work in offline contextual bandits offers a path to personalization, but reward functions for learning outcomes are notoriously difficult to specify and are vulnerable to reward hacking when optimized, potentially harming student engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive intervention policy that dynamically adjusts feedback based on student context, and whose reward function is iteratively repaired using human educator feedback, will yield greater learning gains and engagement than static or context-agnostic adaptive policies.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-phase study. First, we will train an initial policy using offline contextual bandits (e.g., a doubly robust estimator) on historical student interaction data. Second, we will deploy this policy in an A/B test platform (like ASSISTments or a MOOC) where it selects feedback interventions. Third, we will integrate the 'Reward Repair' framework: an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) will synthesize structured educator critiques of policy decisions into proposed reward function modifications, which are then used to update the bandit policy online, mitigating reward drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, deployable pipeline for creating context-sensitive educational policies that are robust to reward misspecification. It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of combining offline bandits with online reward repair in real-world educational settings, advancing both personalized learning and safe reward learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses bandits for customization or LLMs for feedback generation, this uniquely integrates them: using LLMs not as the intervention source, but as a tool to interpret human feedback and repair the bandit's reward function dynamically, addressing contextual heterogeneity and reward hacking simultaneously.

proposals.title: Causal Reward Models for Subgroup-Specific Forecasting of Educational Intervention Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Personalized education aims to match interventions to student needs, but current predictive models often rely on correlational patterns from offline data, which can be biased and fail to generalize. This leads to inefficient resource allocation and potential harm from poorly matched interventions. Developing robust, causal forecasting models is critical for scaling effective, equitable educational policies.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating causal inference with reward modeling, using human feedback to correct for unobserved confounders, will yield more accurate and robust forecasts of intervention efficacy for specific student subgroups than standard supervised learning or bandit approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage model. First, we train a base reward function on historical student interaction data (e.g., log data from tutoring software) to predict long-term outcomes from interventions and student context. Second, we adapt the 'Repairing Reward Functions with Feedback' paradigm, using limited human educator feedback on proposed intervention forecasts to correct the reward model for subgroup-specific biases and hidden confounders. The final forecasting model will be a causal forest or a doubly robust estimator that uses the corrected reward as a pseudo-outcome, enabling estimation of Conditional Average Treatment Effects (CATE) for student subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid causal-reward modeling framework for educational forecasting that is more robust to distributional shift and confounding than prior methods. It will provide a practical tool for educators and policymakers to simulate intervention impacts for specific student populations before deployment, improving decision-making.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in synthesizing reward hacking mitigation techniques from RLHF with causal inference for education, moving beyond standard contextual bandits or LLM-augmented tools to create explicitly causal, feedback-corrected forecasters.

proposals.title: Optimizing Multi-Faceted LLM-Agent Support for Online Learners via Contextual Bandits and Reward Shaping
proposals.motivation: Online learning platforms often rely on generic, light-touch interventions (e.g., simple reminders) which fail to address the diverse and complex needs of learners, leading to high attrition and inequitable outcomes. Pre-2026 advances in LLMs and interactive AI agents (e.g., for behavior change) create an opportunity to design more intensive, personalized support systems, but deploying them effectively and safely at scale remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-faceted support system, where an LLM-augmented agent dynamically selects from a portfolio of intensive interventions (e.g., motivational feedback, cognitive scaffolding, planning assistance), can be optimized using offline contextual bandit methods to significantly improve learning outcomes for diverse student populations without causing harm from poorly-timed interventions.
proposals.method: We will design a system where a student's state (performance, engagement, self-report) is context for a bandit whose arms are intensive support actions generated by a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., based on architectures like Bloom). To address reward misspecification and potential 'reward hacking' by the LLM agent, we will adapt methods from 'Repairing Reward Functions with Feedback' by using periodic human-in-the-loop feedback from instructors to iteratively shape the reward function driving the bandit's policy optimization. The core algorithm will be an offline contextual bandit (e.g., a policy optimizer like POEM) trained on historical interaction logs to learn a safe initial policy, deployed in a phased trial.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for deploying and optimizing multi-faceted, LLM-driven educational interventions, 2) empirical evidence on the efficacy of intensive versus light-touch support for diverse learners, and 3) a methodology for integrating human feedback to safeguard and improve educational reward functions in real-world deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses bandits for customizing single-type feedback or LLMs for static behavior change, this integrates them to dynamically orchestrate a suite of intensive LLM-generated actions, with a novel focus on mitigating reward misspecification risks in educational AI agents via human feedback.

proposals.title: Dynamic Student Engagement Phenotyping for Adaptive Feedback via Offline Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Current educational feedback systems often rely on coarse, infrequent measures of student state, missing critical real-time fluctuations in engagement and affect that impact learning. Digital phenotyping, proven in mobile health for modeling mental states, offers a rich, continuous data stream but has not been systematically applied to create dynamic student profiles for real-time intervention. This gap limits the ability to personalize support at the moment it is most needed, a core challenge in educational policy and technology.
proposals.hypothesis: That continuous sensor data (e.g., phone/computer use patterns, audio prosody, typing dynamics) can be used to infer latent student engagement states, and that feedback policies optimized with respect to these inferred states will lead to improved learning outcomes compared to static or schedule-based interventions.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline to process multimodal sensor data into low-dimensional state representations using time-series models (e.g., LSTMs or transformers). This state representation will serve as input to a contextual bandit or offline reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm, building on prior work like Offline Contextual Multi-Armed Bandits. The action space will consist of feedback interventions (e.g., motivational messages, practice problem hints) delivered via an LLM-augmented interface, inspired by the Bloom framework. We will evaluate policy performance using logged educational interaction data and simulated rollouts, with a reward function carefully designed to balance engagement metrics and learning gains, incorporating techniques from reward function repair to mitigate hacking.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological framework for real-time student phenotyping and state-aware intervention delivery. It will advance the field of educational technology by providing a concrete, data-driven approach to operationalizing continuous engagement, and contribute to RL/bandit literature by addressing the challenge of learning from noisy, high-dimensional observational data in a socially impactful domain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of mobile health digital phenotyping techniques with offline RL for educational feedback, creating a closed-loop system where the student's continuously inferred cognitive-affective state directly shapes the timing and content of adaptive support, moving beyond simple performance-based triggers.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Reward Functions for LLM Tutors with Online Learner Communities
proposals.motivation: Online learning platforms increasingly deploy LLM-powered tutors, but their feedback mechanisms are often designed by developers without direct learner input, risking misalignment with student goals and potential reward hacking. This is critical because poorly specified reward functions can lead to ineffective or even harmful tutoring strategies, undermining educational outcomes. Engaging learners in the design process can create more trustworthy and pedagogically sound AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A participatory design process with online learner communities will yield reward function specifications for LLM tutors that are more robust to reward hacking and lead to higher perceived utility and learning gains compared to expert-designed baselines.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Bloom framework for LLM-augmented behavior change to structure co-design workshops with learners from platforms like edX. Using methods from 'Repairing Reward Functions with Feedback,' we will translate learner narratives and preferences into initial reward function candidates. These will be tested and refined via an offline contextual bandit simulation, modeled on prior work for customizing feedback, which uses historical learner interaction data to evaluate the performance and potential vulnerabilities of each candidate reward specification before live deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for human-in-the-loop reward specification grounded in HCI co-design principles. It will produce concrete, empirically evaluated reward functions for educational LLMs and demonstrate how participatory design can improve the safety and alignment of AI systems in social domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used human feedback to repair rewards or customize interventions, the active co-design of the reward function's *objective* with the end-user community before optimization is a novel synthesis of participatory HCI and reward learning. This pre-emptively addresses misalignment rather than correcting it post-hoc.

proposals.title: Instructor-AI Teaming for Persistence: A Contextual Bandit Framework for Adaptive Support Systems
proposals.motivation: Educational technology often focuses on direct student interventions, but instructors are critical for fostering student persistence and are themselves subject to behavioral biases and limited bandwidth. Pre-2026, scalable tools to help instructors provide timely, effective support are lacking, leading to missed opportunities to improve educational outcomes. This work addresses the gap by designing AI systems that augment, not replace, instructor decision-making to sustain student engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive support system that provides contextually relevant, behaviorally-informed prompts to instructors will increase the frequency and quality of their persistence-supporting interventions, leading to measurable improvements in student engagement metrics compared to static dashboards.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a contextual multi-armed bandit (CMAB) where the context includes student activity, past performance, and instructor state. The 'arms' are a set of pre-designed, evidence-based support actions (e.g., 'send a check-in message to student X', 'form a peer group for topic Y') drawn from behavioral science. The system will use a Thompson Sampling or Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) algorithm to personalize action suggestions to maximize a reward function co-designed with instructors, which balances student response (e.g., re-engagement) and instructor burden. We will deploy and evaluate this system in a controlled online course setting.
proposals.contribution: This contributes a novel computational framework for instructor-in-the-loop educational support, grounded in offline bandit learning to ensure safe personalization. It advances the field of human-AI interaction in education by shifting the focus from student-facing AI to AI-augmented instructor coaching, with explicit modeling of instructor behavior and preferences.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses bandits for direct student interventions, this applies them to optimize *instructor* actions, treating the instructor as a behavioral agent whose support strategies can be adaptively prompted. It uniquely integrates behavioral insight design with robust, feedback-driven online learning for a human-in-the-loop system.

proposals.title: Modeling Non-Stationary Student Engagement with Adaptive, LLM-Augmented Contextual Bandits
proposals.motivation: Educational technologies often assume student engagement and learning processes are stationary, but in reality, they evolve due to factors like skill mastery, fatigue, and changing interests. This stationarity assumption can lead to suboptimal or harmful feedback policies over time, a form of 'reward hacking' where a static model exploits a flawed, unchanging reward function. Grounded in pre-2026 work on mitigating reward hacking and customizing feedback, this proposal addresses the core limitation of static student models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling student engagement as a non-stationary latent process will enable more robust and effective personalized feedback interventions, improving long-term learning outcomes compared to stationary models.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a non-stationary contextual bandit where the reward function, representing engagement or learning gain, evolves. We will adapt algorithms like Discounted Linear Thompson Sampling or sliding-window UCB to track changes. To infer the latent engagement state, we will integrate LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) to process rich, unstructured student interactions (text responses, forum posts) as contextual features, following the 'Bloom' framework for behavior change. The system will be evaluated using high-fidelity educational simulators and historical datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework and algorithms for non-stationary student modeling in education. It will provide empirical evidence on the pitfalls of stationarity and demonstrate improved long-term policy performance. The integration of LLMs for state inference advances methods for leveraging rich human feedback in adaptive systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work customizes feedback, it typically assumes a stationary student model. This proposal uniquely combines non-stationary bandit algorithms with LLM-based state representation specifically to relax this core stationarity assumption in educational contexts.

proposals.title: Safely Improving Educational Policies via Model-Based Offline RL with Agnostic Evaluation and Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) offers a powerful tool for optimizing policies from logged educational data, such as feedback interventions for students. However, deploying policies learned from imperfect, static datasets risks poor performance due to distributional shift and reward mis-specification, which is particularly dangerous in high-stakes educational settings. Pre-2026 work has shown the promise of model-based offline RL (e.g., MOPO, MOReL) for safer policy improvement and the value of human feedback for repairing reward functions, but these threads remain largely separate.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating representation-agnostic policy evaluation (inspired by 'Repairing Reward Functions...') with a learned probabilistic environment model will enable more robust and safer policy improvement from offline educational data, by jointly constraining updates to policies supported by the model and validating them against human feedback signals.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework from 'Repairing Reward Functions...' to the model-based offline RL setting. First, we will train an ensemble of probabilistic dynamics models on the offline dataset of student interactions. We will then use these models to generate synthetic rollouts for candidate policies. Crucially, policy evaluation will employ an agnostic evaluator—a separate model or human-in-the-loop LLM (as in 'Bloom')—to provide feedback on the synthetic trajectories, creating a corrected reward signal. Policy improvement will be constrained by a penalty on states where the dynamics model ensemble has high uncertainty, similar to MOPO, but guided by the agnostic evaluator's feedback to mitigate reward hacking.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid algorithm for safe offline policy optimization in human-centric domains like education. It will provide both theoretical and empirical evidence on how model-based uncertainty quantification and human feedback can be synergistically combined to prevent harmful distributional shift and reward exploitation. The resulting framework will be validated on simulated and real educational intervention datasets.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of representation-agnostic evaluation, typically used for reward correction, directly into the model-based offline RL loop for safety. This moves beyond using feedback solely for pre-training or final validation, instead using it iteratively to guide constrained policy updates within a learned model.

proposals.title: Fairness-Aware Offline Policy Evaluation for Adaptive Educational Interventions
proposals.motivation: Adaptive learning systems and AI-driven educational tools, increasingly powered by LLMs, promise personalized support but risk perpetuating or exacerbating existing inequities. Pre-2026, offline policy evaluation (OPE) methods for contextual bandits in education focus on average performance, lacking systematic audits for disparate impact across student subgroups defined by sensitive attributes like prior achievement or demographic factors. This gap is critical as deploying a policy that is effective on average but harmful to a subgroup could undermine educational equity.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating subgroup-specific fairness metrics into OPE for educational contextual bandits will reveal significant performance disparities in candidate policies that are masked by aggregate metrics, and that a fairness-constrained policy selection criterion can identify policies with a better trade-off between overall reward and equitable outcomes.
proposals.method: We will extend the OPE framework from recent work on customizing feedback with offline bandits. For a logged dataset of student interactions, we will compute standard OPE estimates (e.g., via Inverse Propensity Scoring or Doubly Robust estimators) not just globally but also for predefined subgroups. We will then integrate algorithmic fairness metrics—such as equality of opportunity (matching true positive rates) or demographic parity—as constraints or penalty terms in the policy selection objective. The core algorithm will involve a multi-objective optimization or a constrained optimization step post-evaluation, using pre-2026 fairness toolkits like AIF360 or Fairlearn.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodology for auditing and selecting educational intervention policies for fairness prior to deployment, contributing to the responsible development of AI in education. It will yield a set of fairness-aware OPE metrics and a selection protocol that can be integrated into existing educational data science pipelines, advancing the field beyond aggregate performance.
proposals.novelty: While algorithmic fairness is established in classification, its integration into the OPE stage of contextual bandits for education is novel, moving beyond post-hoc audits of trained models to proactive, pre-deployment fairness assessment of intervention policies. This bridges the distinct pre-2026 literatures on educational bandits and algorithmic fairness in a new, application-critical way.

proposals.title: Meta-Eval: Meta-Learning for Sample-Efficient Off-Policy Evaluation Across Educational Domains
proposals.motivation: Evaluating educational policies, like feedback strategies for different student groups, is crucial for safe deployment but requires costly real-world trials. Pre-2026, offline policy evaluation (OPE) methods struggle with high variance and bias when data is scarce in a new domain (e.g., a new school or subject). This limits the rapid, data-efficient personalization of interventions championed in Brunskill's prior work on contextual bandits and behavior change.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework, trained on historical OPE experiments from diverse educational domains, can learn a prior that enables more accurate and sample-efficient off-policy evaluation in a novel target domain with limited interaction data.
proposals.method: We will frame cross-domain OPE as a meta-learning problem. We will adapt a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework to learn an initial set of parameters for a reward function approximator (e.g., a neural network) from a distribution of source domains, each with its own historical policy deployment data. The inner-loop adaptation will fine-tune this model using the small target domain dataset. We will benchmark against direct OPE estimators (e.g., Doubly Robust, MAGIC) and ablate the meta-learning component, using simulated and real educational datasets (e.g., ASSISTments).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for reducing the data required to reliably evaluate policies in new educational settings, lowering the barrier to evidence-based personalization. It will contribute a new benchmark for cross-domain OPE and extend the meta-learning paradigm from policy optimization to the evaluation setting, which is critical for safe, iterative improvement of LLM-augmented or human-in-the-loop educational systems.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to policy learning, its application to off-policy *evaluation* for cross-domain educational problems is novel. This bridges Brunskill's work on reward hacking mitigation (which requires reliable evaluation) with the practical need for customization across diverse student populations with minimal new data.

proposals.title: Off-Policy Evaluation at Scale: A/B Testing Infrastructure for Real-Time Educational Interventions on Massive Platforms
proposals.motivation: Massive online learning platforms serve millions of students, creating an unprecedented opportunity to personalize education and improve outcomes. However, deploying new teaching policies via traditional A/B testing is slow, risky, and can waste valuable learning opportunities for students in control groups. Pre-2026, offline policy evaluation (OPE) methods offer a safer alternative but have not been rigorously validated or deployed at the scale and real-time decision-making pace required by these platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robust, high-throughput offline evaluation pipeline, leveraging modern contextual bandit and doubly robust estimators, can reliably predict the performance of new educational intervention policies from historical logs, enabling rapid, low-risk policy iteration on platforms with millions of users.
proposals.method: We will build on the offline contextual bandit framework from prior work, scaling it to distributed data systems (e.g., Spark). The pipeline will ingest historical interaction logs (state, action, reward) and implement a suite of OPE estimators, including Inverse Propensity Scoring (IPS), Direct Method (DM), and Doubly Robust (DR) estimators. We will integrate this with a real-time service to score candidate policies (e.g., new feedback delivery rules or LLM-augmented hints) against the logged data, simulating A/B test outcomes. Validation will involve deploying the top-ranked policies in small-scale, staggered live tests to assess the fidelity of the offline predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source, production-tested framework for large-scale OPE in education technology, directly enabling faster and safer educational innovation. It will yield new empirical insights into the practical limitations and scaling properties of OPE methods in high-stakes, non-stationary environments with complex human feedback.
proposals.novelty: While OPE is known in theory, its application to real-time decision-making for millions of students, integrated with modern data infrastructure and validated against live deployments on an educational platform, is a novel and significant engineering and research challenge beyond prior proof-of-concept studies.

proposals.title: Inverse Policy Evaluation for Latent Student Model Inference in Adaptive Educational Systems
proposals.motivation: Adaptive learning systems rely on models of student engagement and learning to personalize feedback and practice. However, these latent student models are often misspecified, leading to suboptimal or even harmful educational policies. Pre-2026 work in offline policy evaluation for education focuses on estimating policy performance, but does not provide a principled way to diagnose and repair the underlying student model when performance is poor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing the problem as an inverse policy evaluation task, we can infer the parameters of a latent student engagement model from observed historical interaction data and policy performance estimates, thereby correcting model misspecification.
proposals.method: We will treat the student's latent state (e.g., engagement, proficiency) as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). Using pre-collected offline interaction logs, we will first perform high-confidence off-policy evaluation (e.g., using Doubly Robust or MAGIC estimators) to assess the performance of a deployed feedback policy. We will then formulate an inverse optimization problem that searches for POMDP transition and reward function parameters that, when used in policy evaluation, align the predicted performance with the empirically estimated performance. This will be solved using gradient-based methods, potentially leveraging LLMs as differentiable simulators for generating plausible student response patterns, as inspired by the 'Bloom' framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for diagnosing and repairing student models in educational technology, moving beyond mere policy assessment to model improvement. It contributes a new 'inverse' perspective to the educational policy evaluation literature and offers a concrete tool for creating more robust, student-aligned adaptive systems.
proposals.novelty: While inverse reinforcement learning infers rewards from optimal behavior, our approach inversely infers general latent model parameters from policy performance data, a distinct problem tailored for educational settings where optimal policies are unknown. This connects offline policy evaluation directly to model-based learning in a novel way.

proposals.title: Adaptive Uniform-PAC Guarantees via Online Learning Reductions: Applications to Safe Educational Policy Learning
proposals.motivation: Uniform-PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) RL algorithms provide strong, anytime learning guarantees crucial for safety-critical domains like educational policy optimization, where a student's learning trajectory must be protected from poor exploration. However, existing Uniform-PAC methods often rely on worst-case, non-adaptive confidence intervals, leading to sample-inefficient exploration. This inefficiency limits their practical deployment in settings with human feedback, where data is scarce and costly.
proposals.hypothesis: By reframing Uniform-PAC RL as an online learning problem over confidence sets, we can import adaptive regret minimization tools (e.g., optimistic online mirror descent, adaptive regret bounds) to dynamically tighten confidence intervals, leading to significantly improved sample complexity while preserving uniform learning guarantees.
proposals.method: We will construct a reduction from Uniform-PAC exploration in finite-horizon MDPs to a linear online learning problem over the space of plausible models (confidence sets). The core algorithm will maintain an evolving confidence set via an adaptive online learner that updates based on observed state transitions and rewards. Specifically, we will adapt the 'ORLC' (Optimistic RL with Confidence sets) framework, replacing its static confidence radii with those derived from an adaptive online learner (e.g., a variant of Follow-the-Regularized-Leader with adaptive step-sizes). We will analyze the resulting algorithm's Uniform-PAC sample complexity and validate it in simulated educational policy environments with synthetic student models.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new class of sample-efficient Uniform-PAC RL algorithms with adaptive confidence tuning. It will provide a formal bridge between adaptive online learning theory and safe RL exploration. The resulting methods will be directly applicable to domains like intelligent tutoring systems, where policies must learn safely from limited student interaction data.
proposals.novelty: While reductions exist for standard regret minimization, applying adaptive online learning tools specifically to tighten Uniform-PAC guarantees is novel. This approach moves beyond static concentration inequalities to leverage the sequential structure of data for more efficient, yet still provably safe, exploration.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Uniform-PAC Adaptation in Interactive Educational Systems
proposals.motivation: In domains like intelligent tutoring systems, where a policy must learn from and adapt to new students, we need guarantees of both good average performance (PAC) and uniform performance across all time steps (Uniform-PAC) to ensure safety and avoid catastrophic failures. Current Uniform-PAC algorithms are sample-inefficient when facing new tasks from a known distribution, limiting their practical use in personalized education. This work addresses the need for fast, reliable adaptation with strong theoretical guarantees in human-in-the-loop learning scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: By meta-learning a prior over exploration strategies from a distribution of related interactive tasks (e.g., different student cohorts), an agent can achieve Uniform-PAC guarantees on a new, unseen task from the same distribution with significantly fewer samples than learning from scratch.
proposals.method: We will extend the Uniform-PAC framework for finite-horizon MDPs, as formalized by Dann et al. (2017) and Modi et al. (2021), by integrating a meta-learning outer loop. Using a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) style approach, we will train a model initialization on a set of training MDPs representing different student interaction environments. The inner loop will perform a few steps of a Uniform-PAC algorithm (e.g., UBEV). We will theoretically analyze the sample complexity of the adapted policy and empirically validate the method in simulated educational bandit and MDP environments derived from student interaction logs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first sample-complexity bounds for achieving Uniform-PAC via meta-learning, bridging theoretical safe RL and practical few-shot adaptation. It will deliver a practical algorithm for building rapidly adaptive, yet provably reliable, tutoring systems and feedback policies, directly contributing to the design of safe LLM-augmented behavior change interactions.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning for sample-efficient PAC guarantees exists, its application to the stricter Uniform-PAC criterion—which prevents even temporary bad performance—is unexplored. This combines the safety focus of Brunskill's reward hacking mitigation work with the task-adaptation efficiency of meta-learning for educational contexts.

proposals.title: Uniform-PAC Guarantees for Off-Policy Evaluation: A Framework for Reliable Policy Assessment with Limited Data
proposals.motivation: Off-policy evaluation (OPE) is critical for safely assessing educational or behavioral policies from logged data, as in Brunskill's work on feedback interventions. Existing high-probability guarantees for OPE are often one-shot, providing confidence for a single, fixed evaluation. This is insufficient for adaptive systems that must evaluate many candidate policies or for practitioners who need guarantees that hold uniformly across all evaluations in a sequence, a common requirement for robust decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Uniform-PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) framework, recently developed for online reinforcement learning, can be successfully adapted to OPE, providing guarantees that the value estimates for all policies in a (potentially infinite) class are accurate with high probability, for all sample sizes simultaneously.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Uniform-PAC framework, as formalized by Dann et al. (2017) and others for control, to the problem of policy evaluation. The core technical approach will involve: 1) Defining a Uniform-PAC sample complexity for OPE, where the number of episodes needed to estimate the value of all policies in a class to ε-accuracy with probability 1-δ is bounded. 2) Developing and analyzing algorithms based on concentration inequalities and complexity measures (e.g., bracketing entropy) of the policy class. 3) Focusing initially on finite-horizon MDPs with known dynamics but unknown rewards, using importance sampling or model-based estimators as the base, then extending to unknown dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first Uniform-PAC sample complexity bounds for OPE, offering a stronger, more practical guarantee for sequential policy assessment. It will yield a theoretically grounded framework for determining when logged educational interaction data is sufficient to reliably evaluate a set of feedback policies, directly informing the design of more robust offline contextual bandit systems for education.
proposals.novelty: While Uniform-PAC is established for online RL control, its application to the *evaluation* problem is novel. This shifts the focus from guaranteeing learning progress to guaranteeing assessment reliability, a distinct challenge requiring new analysis centered on estimation rather than exploration.

proposals.title: When is Dialogue Necessary? Mapping Transfer Learning Failures in LLMs to Educational Engagement Requirements
proposals.motivation: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) in education often assumes a conversational interface is universally beneficial for engagement. However, adapting foundation models to specific educational domains via fine-tuning or prompting can lead to unpredictable performance degradation (transfer failures), especially in complex pedagogical tasks. This creates a critical design and cost-efficiency problem: building full chatbots may be unnecessary where models transfer robustly, and insufficient where they fail, potentially harming student learning.
proposals.hypothesis: The necessity of a conversational interface for effective educational engagement is predictable and is most critical in task domains where adapted LLMs exhibit significant transfer learning failures, particularly for pedagogically complex skills like conceptual reasoning and misconception debugging.
proposals.method: We will first adapt a pre-trained LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2 or GPT-3.5) to a programming or science education domain using standard fine-tuning and instruction-tuning. We will then systematically evaluate its performance across a spectrum of tasks (e.g., syntax correction, conceptual Q&A, debugging flawed logic) to map transfer failure modes. For each task, we will conduct a randomized controlled trial with students, comparing engagement and learning outcomes from a full conversational tutor against a constrained interface (e.g., multiple-choice with static feedback). We will use offline contextual bandit methods to analyze the interaction data and model the conditional necessity of dialogue as a function of task complexity and model performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a predictive framework linking model capability gaps to educational interface design, offering a principled, evidence-based guide for when to invest in conversational AI for learning. It will contribute a novel methodology for evaluating educational AI that jointly considers algorithmic performance and human learning needs, advancing the field beyond heuristic design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies transfer learning or educational interfaces in isolation, this research explicitly connects model failure modes to human-in-the-loop engagement requirements. It challenges the default assumption that 'more conversational is always better' by empirically testing when simpler, cheaper interactions suffice or fail, grounded in the model's own competency.

proposals.title: Auditing Pedagogical Policies: A Decentralized Framework for Composing and Evaluating LLM-Based Tutoring Strategies
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of LLMs as tutors creates a critical problem of pedagogical opacity and centralization. Educators and researchers cannot audit or modify the core teaching logic of proprietary systems, hindering scientific verification of educational claims and preventing customization for diverse student needs. An open, modular framework is needed to democratize the development and evaluation of AI teaching strategies, aligning with calls for transparency and safety in AI policy.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized framework for composing pedagogical models as explicit, auditable policy modules will enable more effective, verifiable, and adaptable LLM-based tutoring by facilitating community-driven development, testing, and refinement of tutorial strategies.
proposals.method: We will design a framework where a tutoring agent's behavior is governed by a pipeline of discrete, interchangeable modules (e.g., misconception detector, hint generator, feedback selector) with standardized interfaces. Each module is a 'pedagogical policy' that can be implemented via rule sets, fine-tuned small models, or prompts for a backbone LLM. We will develop a specification language for these modules and a runtime that composes them. Evaluation will use offline contextual bandit methods on historical interaction logs to assess the performance of different module combinations, and online A/B testing with simulated and human students to measure learning gains.
proposals.contribution: 1) A technical framework and open-source prototype for building auditable, composable cognitive tutors. 2) A methodology for offline and online evaluation of pedagogical policy modules, extending reward design and policy learning to education. 3) An initial repository of benchmarked modules, creating a foundation for a community-driven 'marketplace' of teaching strategies.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond treating the tutor as a monolithic LLM by decomposing it into auditable, swappable policy components, applying principles of AI safety and reward auditing specifically to the pedagogical domain. It fuses ideas from modular ITS architecture with modern LLM-augmented interaction design and offline policy evaluation.

proposals.title: Auditing and Repairing Reward Functions for Equitable Personalization in LLM-Based Educational Dialogue
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of LLM-based educational dialogue agents risks encoding societal biases into their adaptive feedback, potentially exacerbating inequities for students from diverse cultural, linguistic, or socioeconomic backgrounds. Pre-2026, while personalization improves average learning gains, methods to audit and ensure these systems do not cause disparate outcomes or enable manipulative practices are critically underdeveloped. This work addresses a core societal risk in the scaling of AI-augmented education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reward functions and feedback policies driving personalization in educational LLMs can be systematically audited for disparate impact, and that these functions can be algorithmically repaired using constrained optimization and human-in-the-loop feedback to ensure equitable adaptation without sacrificing overall learning performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Reward Repair' framework to the educational domain, treating the LLM's dialogue strategy as a policy optimized for a latent reward function inferred from human feedback or performance data. Using offline contextual bandit methods, we will audit for disparities in suggested interventions across student subgroups. We will then formulate and solve a constrained policy optimization problem, where the reward function is repaired to satisfy fairness constraints (e.g., demographic parity in engagement or help requests) while maintaining high overall learning gains, validated via simulated and real-world student interaction logs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a technical toolkit for auditing bias in adaptive educational dialogue policies, 2) a novel method for 'reward repair' in interactive learning systems that balances personalization with equity, and 3) an empirical evaluation of the trade-offs between adaptive efficacy and fairness in simulated and real educational settings.
proposals.novelty: This uniquely fuses reward hacking mitigation techniques from safe RL with equity-focused educational data mining, moving beyond static bias detection in datasets to actively repairing the dynamic, interactive policies of LLM-based tutors—a novel application of constrained policy optimization for algorithmic fairness in education.

proposals.title: Temporal Audit Trails for LLM Knowledge Consolidation: A Discovery-Based Framework for Measuring Risk in Extended Interactions
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are deployed in extended, multi-session interactions (e.g., tutoring, therapy), there is a critical gap in understanding how prolonged user dialogue shapes the model's internal knowledge and risk profile. Pre-2026, auditing focuses on static training data or final outputs, missing the dynamic process where user-provided information may be integrated, potentially leading to unintended memorization of sensitive or copyrighted content. This creates legal and ethical blind spots for long-term AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: Extended, multi-turn interactions with a user lead to systematic, traceable consolidation of user-provided knowledge in an LLM's parameters, and the structure of this consolidation—measurable via discovery-inspired audit tools—predicts the risk of unintended memorization and recall more accurately than static model snapshots.
proposals.method: We will adapt concepts from legal e-discovery to design a controlled audit environment. Using a base LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2 or GPT-3.5 Turbo API), we will simulate extended tutoring-style dialogues where a 'student' agent introduces specific knowledge 'artifacts' (e.g., fictional copyrighted passages, private details). We will employ probing techniques, gradient-based attribution methods (e.g., TracIn), and mechanistic interpretability tools to create temporal traces of how these artifacts are encoded after each interaction session. We will model the consolidation process using offline contextual bandits to analyze how dialogue patterns (duration, repetition, questioning style) influence encoding efficiency and fragility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for dynamically auditing AI learning processes, moving beyond static benchmarks. It will produce concrete metrics for 'knowledge consolidation risk' tied to interaction patterns, directly informing policy for long-term AI deployment. The methodology bridges ML auditing with human-computer interaction, offering tools for developers to monitor and potentially intervene in real-time learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work audits training data or repairs reward functions, this proposal uniquely applies a discovery-process lens to the *interactive learning phase* of a deployed model. It fuses temporal trace analysis from HCI with mechanistic interpretability to create a dynamic risk model that evolves with use, a non-obvious synthesis of legal, educational, and ML safety methodologies.

proposals.title: Strategic Adaptation in AI Tutors: Learning to Select Pedagogical Dialogue Strategies from Micro-Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current LLM-based tutors, trained on vast web corpora, often generate pedagogically unsound or generic dialogue, lacking the nuanced strategies expert human tutors use to adapt to individual learners. This limits their educational effectiveness and can lead to negative learning experiences. A method is needed to ground tutor dialogue generation in high-quality, proven instructional interactions rather than statistically common text.
proposals.hypothesis: An AI tutor can learn to dynamically select and apply distinct, high-quality pedagogical dialogue strategies (e.g., Socratic questioning, motivational feedback) by training on small, annotated micro-corpora of expert human tutoring, leading to more effective and personalized learning interactions than models trained on generic dialogue data.
proposals.method: We will curate micro-corpora of tutoring dialogues, each tagged with a pedagogical strategy, coarse learner state, and outcome (e.g., confusion resolved). We will train a two-stage model: first, a context encoder maps the interaction history to a latent state; second, a policy network, framed as a contextual bandit, selects a strategy. The selected strategy conditions a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., Llama-2) to generate the next utterance. We will train the policy using offline bandit learning algorithms (e.g., Fitted Q-Evaluation with Double Robustness) on the annotated corpora, with the reward function defined by annotated outcomes and human feedback on dialogue quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for data-efficient, strategy-grounded dialogue training for AI tutors, moving beyond next-token prediction on web data. It will contribute a novel offline bandit learning formulation for pedagogical strategy selection and demonstrate how small, high-quality datasets can steer LLMs toward more effective and adaptive educational behaviors.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines micro-corpora curation with offline contextual bandit learning to explicitly model and optimize the *selection* of pedagogical strategies, a level of control absent in end-to-end fine-tuning on mixed-quality data. It directly connects the 'Bloom' framework for LLM-augmented behavior change to concrete, learnable tutoring policies.

proposals.title: Federated Learning of Pedagogical Policies for LLM-Based Tutors with Privacy-Preserving Reward Shaping
proposals.motivation: Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS) require vast, diverse student interaction data to learn effective pedagogical strategies, but such data is highly sensitive and siloed across institutions. Centralizing this data for training large-scale dialogue-based tutors poses significant privacy and logistical barriers. Federated learning offers a path to leverage this distributed data, but standard methods are not designed for the structured pedagogical scaffolding and reward-based optimization central to ITS.
proposals.hypothesis: A federated learning framework that treats local ITS scaffolding as a source of implicit reward feedback can learn a global, high-performing tutoring policy that discovers and encodes effective pedagogical strategies and common student misconception patterns, while preserving data privacy.
proposals.method: We propose a federated policy optimization method where each local node (e.g., a school cluster) hosts an LLM-based dialogue tutor, scaffolded by a local cognitive model (e.g., Bayesian Knowledge Tracing). Local training uses interaction sequences to define a reward function—combining student correctness, hint usage, and learning gains—to fine-tune the tutor via reinforcement learning (e.g., PPO). A central server aggregates policy parameter updates (using secure aggregation like FedAvg) to produce a global tutor. We will adapt reward-shaping and repair techniques to align local rewards during federation, mitigating policy divergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel federated RL algorithm for training pedagogical dialogue agents with privacy guarantees, 2) empirical evidence on the emergent discovery of tutoring strategies from decentralized data, and 3) an open-source framework for federated ITS development, advancing the practical deployment of personalized, data-driven tutors.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of federated learning with structured ITS scaffolding and reward-based policy optimization for dialogue agents is novel, moving beyond simple model aggregation to the privacy-preserving learning of complex pedagogical decision-making policies.

proposals.title: Fractionating Interactivity: Isolating the Minimal Sufficient Components of LLM-Dialogue for Learning
proposals.motivation: LLM-powered chatbots are increasingly deployed in education, often with the assumption that open-ended dialogue is the key driver of engagement and learning gains. However, building and maintaining full dialogue systems is resource-intensive, and their complexity obscures which interactive elements are truly necessary. This work addresses a critical gap in the design of AI for education by moving beyond monolithic 'chatbot vs. control' comparisons to identify the core interactive mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: The measurable benefits of an LLM-based educational dialogue system can be replicated or even exceeded by a significantly simplified system composed of specific, non-conversational interactive components (e.g., structured choice, reactive feedback), which constitute the 'active ingredient' for learning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the pharmacological fractionation paradigm to an educational technology context. First, we will decompose a state-of-the-art instruction-tuned LLM (e.g., Llama 2-Chat) used as a full dialogue tutor into distinct functional components: a multiple-choice prompt engine, a reactive feedback generator, and a scaffolded input (sentence starter) provider. Using an offline contextual bandit framework, we will design a multi-arm experiment where these components are tested in isolation and in combination against the full dialogue and a static text control. The primary metrics will be engagement (e.g., time-on-task, interaction frequency) and learning (pre/post-test gains), analyzed to identify the minimal sufficient interactive 'treatment.'
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for rigorously deconstructing complex AI interactions. It will yield concrete, efficient design principles for building effective educational tools, moving the field from 'whether' interactivity helps to 'what kind' and 'how much' is needed. The results could inform more scalable and interpretable policy for deploying AI in classrooms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares full systems, this is the first to systematically apply a pharmacological decomposition analogy to isolate the causal components of LLM-based interactivity. It uniquely combines concepts from reward shaping (e.g., feedback as a reward signal) and bandit-based personalization within a fractionation experimental design.

proposals.title: Scaffolding Without Dialogue: Evaluating Engagement and Learning in Complex, Multi-Step AI-Human Problem Solving
proposals.motivation: Current research on human-AI interaction, especially in education, heavily focuses on dialogic interfaces like chatbots and tutors. However, many high-value cognitive tasks, such as scientific modeling or debugging, are inherently non-conversational but could still benefit from AI scaffolding. It is unclear whether the benefits of AI collaboration stem from conversational rapport or from structured support for complex joint reasoning, a distinction critical for designing effective, scalable educational and decision-support tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an AI agent can effectively scaffold deep user engagement and learning in complex, multi-step tasks through structured, non-dialogic interactions, and that the resulting learning gains and engagement metrics will be comparable to or greater than those from conversational interfaces for the same tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a suite of complex, interactive tasks (e.g., causal model construction, simulation debugging) deployed via a web platform. A pre-trained LLM (e.g., GPT-4) will be fine-tuned to generate dynamic, context-aware scaffolding (hints, resource suggestions, step decomposition) based on user actions within the task interface, not natural language dialogue. We will evaluate using an offline contextual bandit framework to personalize scaffolding policies from logged interaction data, measuring outcomes via pre/post-test learning gains, interaction logs (time-on-task, depth of exploration), and user self-reports on engagement and cognitive load.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel evaluation framework and benchmark for non-dialogic AI-human collaboration, 2) empirical evidence on the mechanisms of engagement in AI-supported complex reasoning, and 3) new methods for personalizing non-conversational AI support using offline bandit learning, extending the 'customizing feedback' theme from prior work to a new interaction paradigm.
proposals.novelty: This shifts the focus from dialogue as the primary medium of AI-human collaboration to structured, joint activity in a shared task space, using the complexity of the task itself as an experimental probe to dissect engagement. It combines scalable LLM-based scaffolding with rigorous, personalized policy evaluation from the bandit literature in a novel setting.

proposals.title: Disentangling Engagement: A Causal Mediation Analysis of Interaction Quality, Time, and Learning in LLM-Powered Tutoring
proposals.motivation: Educational chatbots powered by large language models (LLMs) are proliferating, with engagement time often used as a proxy for effectiveness. However, pre-2026 research in intelligent tutoring systems and human-computer interaction suggests that the quality of cognitive processes during practice is a critical mediator of learning. It is unknown whether time-on-task with an LLM tutor drives learning directly or merely correlates with deeper, more productive interactions, a confusion that hinders the design of effective educational policies and feedback systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The positive association between interaction duration and learning gains is not direct but is fully mediated by measurable, high-quality cognitive behaviors (e.g., self-explanation, debugging) during the chat. Simply increasing time without fostering these quality mediators will not improve outcomes.
proposals.method: We will deploy an LLM-based tutoring chatbot for a defined STEM topic (e.g., introductory programming) with logging to capture fine-grained interaction sequences. Using pre-2026 NLP techniques (e.g., supervised classifiers or few-shot prompting with GPT-4), we will code each student utterance and chatbot turn for cognitive behaviors (rehearsal, elaboration, misconception, correction). We will then employ offline causal mediation analysis, building on the potential outcomes framework, to statistically decompose the total effect of session duration on pre-post assessment scores into direct and indirect effects mediated by the quality metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a causal, mechanistic account of how LLM tutoring interactions produce learning, moving beyond correlational claims. It will yield concrete design principles for 'reward functions' in educational LLMs that optimize for quality interactions rather than mere engagement time, directly informing the 'Repairing Reward Functions' line of research. The methodology bridges detailed interaction analytics with robust causal inference for educational technology evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links engagement to outcomes, this study explicitly tests a causal mediation model to disentangle time from interaction quality, a critical step for theory. Furthermore, it adapts causal inference methods from policy evaluation and bandit feedback literature to the novel domain of unstructured LLM-human educational dialogues.

proposals.title: Decomposing Chatbot Interaction Time: A Reward-Based Framework for Distinguishing Deep Processing from Inefficient Looping
proposals.motivation: Educational chatbots promise scalable, personalized learning support, but a core assumption is that increased interaction time correlates with deeper learning. Pre-2026, LLM-augmented systems like Bloom show potential for behavior change, yet they risk inefficient or unproductive interactions that waste student time and could even reinforce misconceptions. Understanding how time is used is critical for designing effective educational policies and interventions that genuinely improve outcomes, not just engagement metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increased total interaction time with an educational chatbot is not uniformly beneficial; rather, learning gains are positively predicted by time segments characterized by linguistic markers of deep processing (e.g., explanation, synthesis) and negatively predicted by segments marked by inefficient interaction patterns (e.g., repetitive clarification loops, off-topic digressions).
proposals.method: We will deploy a tutoring chatbot for a programming or science domain, instrumented to log fine-grained interaction sequences. We will treat the identification of productive vs. unproductive time segments as a reward learning problem. Building on pre-2026 methods like 'Repairing Reward Functions with Feedback,' we will train a classifier (e.g., a transformer-based sequence model) to predict expert-annotated 'deep processing' rewards from interaction features (text coherence, question complexity, error patterns). We will then use offline contextual bandit methods, aligning with Brunskill's prior work, to analyze how different student contexts correlate with the prevalence of these time segments and their ultimate impact on post-test performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, fine-grained analytical framework for evaluating educational chatbot efficacy beyond simple time-on-task. It will contribute a method for automatically diagnosing interaction inefficiencies and a validated set of interaction features that signal meaningful learning. The findings will inform the design of more effective LLM-augmented tutors that can dynamically intervene to steer students towards deeper processing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links overall time to learning, we decompose time using a reward-based, sequence-modeling approach to isolate the *quality* of interaction. This moves beyond correlational studies to a causal-analytical framework that identifies specific, actionable interaction patterns that aid or hinder learning.

proposals.title: Optimizing Knowledge Utility via Interleaved Recall-Application Bandits: A Study in Robust Factual Learning
proposals.motivation: Educational technology often treats factual recall and knowledge application as separate phases, potentially leading to inert knowledge that fails to transfer. This is a critical policy and learning problem, as students need robust, flexible knowledge for real-world tasks. Pre-2026 work on feedback and personalization in education, including Brunskill's research on contextual bandits for feedback, provides a foundation but hasn't systematically optimized the *sequence* of practice types for utility.
proposals.hypothesis: A learning protocol that interleaves pure recall trials with immediate application trials (e.g., inference, problem-solving) will yield superior long-term retention and transfer of factual knowledge compared to blocked practice, and the optimal interleaving schedule can be personalized using learner context.
proposals.method: We will design an online learning platform where factual knowledge units are paired with application micro-tasks. We will model the sequencing problem as a contextual multi-armed bandit, where arms represent different interleaving policies (e.g., recall-application ratio, delay). The reward function will be a composite of retention (delayed test accuracy), access speed, and transfer performance, measured via novel inference problems. We will run controlled experiments comparing fixed schedules against a bandit algorithm that personalizes the sequence based on learner performance and engagement feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational framework for optimizing practice sequences toward knowledge utility, not just recall. It will contribute a novel reward function for educational bandits that balances multiple learning objectives and offer empirical evidence on the efficacy of interleaved recall-application cycles for robust learning.
proposals.novelty: While spaced repetition and interleaving are known, optimizing the *interleaving of recall with application* as a bandit problem to maximize transfer is novel. It moves beyond optimizing for performance on a single task (recall) to optimizing for a composite reward of retention, speed, and transfer, aligning with recent pre-2026 interests in repairing reward functions for complex objectives.

proposals.title: Optimizing Factual Knowledge Interventions: A Contextual Bandit Framework for Variable Practice
proposals.motivation: Educational technologies often optimize for short-term recall, using repetitive Q&A, which can lead to brittle knowledge that fails to transfer. This mirrors the reward hacking problem in RL, where an agent exploits a narrow reward function. We need learning policies that promote robust, long-term knowledge utility, not just immediate test performance, aligning with the challenge of designing effective feedback for students and educators.
proposals.hypothesis: Factual knowledge interventions composed of variable practice types (e.g., explanation, elaboration, application) will induce a more robust and transferable knowledge representation than blocked recall practice, leading to superior long-term retention and flexible use, even if initial accuracy is lower.
proposals.method: We frame the selection of practice type for a given fact and student as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem. The policy, learned via offline bandit algorithms like Doubly Robust Policy Evaluation and optimization, maps student context (knowledge state, past performance) to a practice 'arm' (recall, explain, elaborate, apply). We will conduct a randomized controlled trial in an online learning platform, deploying the learned policy against a standard blocked recall baseline, measuring outcomes on immediate quizzes, delayed tests, and novel application tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven method for personalizing factual learning that moves beyond recall optimization. It contributes a novel application of offline bandit methods to the core problem of knowledge representation design and offers concrete evidence on whether variable practice structures can mitigate 'reward hacking' in educational feedback loops.
proposals.novelty: While variable practice is known in motor learning, its systematic application and optimization for factual knowledge using human-in-the-loop bandit algorithms is novel. It challenges the direct analogy between AI reward maximization and student performance maximization by seeking a policy for robust learning, not just immediate reward.

proposals.title: Optimizing Factual Learning Strategies via Neural-AI Representational Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current educational interventions often rely on coarse behavioral outcomes (e.g., test scores) to evaluate learning strategies, lacking insight into the underlying cognitive mechanisms. This makes it difficult to personalize instruction or design robust AI tutors. Pre-2026 advances in neural decoding and AI interpretability offer a new path: directly comparing how knowledge representations evolve in the human brain and in AI models during learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Learning strategies that induce similar trajectories of representational change in both neural data (fMRI/EEG) and analogous artificial neural networks (ANNs) will be more effective for long-term factual retention, as this alignment signals the induction of stable, generalizable memory representations.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI data from participants learning factual pairs under different strategies (e.g., passive reading, active recall, self-explanation). Using Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA), we will track the evolution of neural representations across sessions. Concurrently, we will train transformer-based models (e.g., a small GPT architecture) on analogous factual datasets using reinforcement learning objectives that mimic each human strategy. We will compute RSA between the neural and model internal representations over simulated learning steps, using this alignment as a reward signal within a contextual bandit framework to optimize the sequence of strategies presented to new learners.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, neurally-grounded computational model of factual learning that explains *why* certain strategies work. It will also deliver a practical, adaptive tutoring policy that selects learning interventions based on predicted representational alignment, moving beyond black-box recommendations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses behavioral data or neural data alone, this proposal fuses them by using AI models as a *computational bridge* to interpret neural representational dynamics, enabling the derivation of learning policies from first-principles representational goals.

proposals.title: From Preference to Habit: A Longitudinal, Context-Aware Study of Casual Learning App Engagement Using Ecological Momentary Assessment and Offline Policy Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Educational technologies often rely on self-reported user preferences for 'casual' learning, but this is a poor predictor of long-term adoption, leading to high attrition and wasted intervention potential. This mirrors a known problem in health psychology, where stated intent for light exercise fails to translate into sustained behavior. A rigorous, data-driven understanding of how casual learning integrates—or fails to integrate—into daily routines is crucial for designing effective, habit-forming learning tools.
proposals.hypothesis: Casual learning app engagement is driven not by initial preference alone, but by the formation of context-dependent habits, where specific environmental or digital cues reliably trigger micro-learning sessions. We hypothesize that interventions (e.g., notifications) can be optimized as contextual bandit policies to maximize the formation of these stable engagement habits.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-week longitudinal field study with a language learning app, instrumented for passive logging of micro-session timing, duration, and device context. We will deploy Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via triggered surveys to measure self-reported habit strength (using the Self-Report Habit Index) and cognitive load in situ. To identify effective facilitators, we will frame notification timing and content as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem, using an offline policy evaluation method (like Fitted Q-Evaluation on logged data) to assess which contextual policies (e.g., morning commute vs. evening break) correlate with increased habit strength and sustained use.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first objective, longitudinal evidence linking micro-behaviors in casual learning to habit formation, moving beyond lab-based self-reports. It will yield a validated model of contextual barriers and facilitators for sustained engagement and demonstrate how offline bandit evaluation can be used to optimize for long-term habit formation, not just short-term clicks.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the rigorous longitudinal methodology of health psychology (EMA) and the policy optimization lens from contextual bandits to the problem of learning technology adoption, creating a novel framework to test if casual learning can become habitual rather than just preferred.

proposals.title: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Sustained Engagement in Casual Learning via LLM-Augmented Adaptive Feedback
proposals.motivation: AI systems for casual learning often fail to sustain user engagement over time, as static personalization cannot adapt to evolving user states and preferences. This limits their long-term educational impact and adoption. A principled approach is needed to dynamically align system behavior with the behavioral outcome of habitual use, preventing disengagement.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling sustained user engagement as a control objective and using formal feedback loops to dynamically adjust interaction parameters (e.g., task difficulty, feedback style) will lead to significantly longer and more stable learning trajectories compared to standard contextual bandit or static personalization methods.
proposals.method: We will frame the user's predicted engagement state as a dynamical system. A high-level controller, informed by a reward function learned from user feedback (inspired by 'Repairing Reward Functions'), will set targets for engagement. A low-level policy, implemented as an offline contextual bandit (extending 'Customizing Feedback Interventions'), will select concrete interventions (e.g., from an LLM-augmented toolkit like 'Bloom') to track this target. The system will use safety filters to mitigate reward hacking of the engagement signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel control-theoretic paradigm for AI alignment focused on long-term behavioral outcomes in human-in-the-loop systems. It will yield a concrete, testable framework for sustainable engagement in educational technology, advancing beyond myopic optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally treating sustained engagement as a control problem to be stabilized, rather than a metric to be myopically maximized, and in the hierarchical integration of learned reward functions, offline bandits, and LLM-augmented interventions within this control loop.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Repairing Fragile Mental Models via Adversarial Dialogue Probes in LLM-Augmented Tutoring
proposals.motivation: Intelligent tutoring systems often fail to ensure robust, transferable understanding, as students can learn superficial patterns from examples without forming correct conceptual models. This is a critical problem in education, where fragile understanding leads to poor real-world application. Pre-2026, LLMs offer new opportunities for interactive dialogue but lack principled methods to diagnose and repair specific student misconceptions.
proposals.hypothesis: An LLM-augmented tutoring system that interleaves core instruction with strategically generated adversarial assessment probes (e.g., counterfactuals, edge cases) can more accurately diagnose gaps in a learner's mental model and, through tailored corrective dialogue, produce conceptual understanding that transfers more effectively to novel problems than standard instructional dialogue.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-phase dialogue agent. First, a core instructional LLM (e.g., fine-tuned from GPT-4) teaches a concept. Second, an adversarial probe generator, framed as a contextual bandit problem, selects from a space of counterfactual scenarios and constraints to challenge the student's stated reasoning. The student's responses are analyzed using a reward function trained on human expert labels of misconception types (e.g., over-generalization). This reward signal guides the bandit to select optimal probes for diagnosis and subsequently triggers a tailored repair dialogue from the instructional LLM, creating a closed-loop feedback system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework for assessing and scaffolding transferable conceptual knowledge through adversarial interaction. It will yield new insights into using online bandit algorithms for personalized educational feedback and demonstrate a concrete method for mitigating reward hacking in AI tutoring, where the 'reward' is robust student understanding rather than superficial task performance.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal integration of adversarial testing as an online, interactive assessment tool within a teaching dialogue, moving beyond static quizzes or example-based instruction.
