This approach directly operationalizes the diagnosis and repair of mental models, a core challenge in learning sciences, using contemporary RL/bandit and LLM methods.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Bypassing Transfer Failure Modes for Efficient Conversational Pedagogy
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) in conversational learning, such as for tutoring or behavior change, often relies on adapting foundation models via fine-tuning or prompting. However, pre-2026 evidence shows such adaptation can fail or degrade performance in specific pedagogical contexts, leading to inefficient or harmful interactions. This is critical because inefficient learning dialogues waste learner time and can undermine educational outcomes, yet there is no systematic framework to predict and circumvent these adaptation failure modes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficiency of a conversational pedagogical agent can be maximized not by generic model compression, but by first diagnosing the specific conditions (e.g., task, learner profile, domain) where standard LLM adaptation fails, and then using this diagnosis to route interactions to specialized, robust protocols that avoid these pitfalls.
proposals.method: We will first construct a diagnostic benchmark of conversational pedagogy tasks, varying learner simulated profiles (e.g., prior knowledge, engagement) and knowledge domains. Using offline contextual bandit methods, we will analyze the performance of fine-tuned and prompted LLMs to map failure modes. This map will then guide the design of a hybrid system that, for a given learner context, selects an efficient interaction protocol—such as constrained rule-based dialogue, strategic few-shot prompting, or a fallback to a smaller specialized model—using a learned routing function. The system will be evaluated via human-in-the-loop studies measuring learning gain per unit time.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a diagnostic framework and benchmark for evaluating transfer learning failures in conversational pedagogy, and 2) a novel efficiency-aware system architecture that uses failure mode diagnosis to dynamically select robust interaction protocols, advancing beyond one-size-fits-all LLM adaptation. It will provide concrete design principles for building time-efficient and robust educational AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores feedback for reward repair or customizing interventions, the novelty lies in proactively diagnosing and circumventing LLM adaptation failures for pedagogical efficiency, framing efficiency as a problem of robust policy selection informed by a map of transfer learning pitfalls, rather than model compression.

proposals.title: EfficientEd: Optimizing Conversational Learning with LLM-Aware Multi-Armed Bandits for Time-Cost Reduction
proposals.motivation: Conversational learning with Large Language Models (LLMs) offers personalized instruction but is often time-intensive, creating a barrier to scalable educational deployment. This inefficiency is critical in real-world settings like classrooms or tutoring, where both student engagement and educator time are limited resources. Pre-2026 work on feedback optimization and behavior change (e.g., Bloom) has not systematically addressed the trade-off between conversational length, learning effectiveness, and user engagement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an LLM-augmented contextual bandit framework can learn to dynamically select or generate conversation interventions (e.g., feedback type, question depth, summarization) that significantly reduce interaction time while preserving, or even enhancing, learning outcomes and engagement compared to fixed or exhaustive conversational policies.
proposals.method: We will adapt offline contextual multi-armed bandit methods, building on prior work in customizing feedback for educators. The 'arms' will be conversation strategies (e.g., direct correction, Socratic questioning, meta-cognitive prompt) parameterized for brevity. Features will include student performance history, engagement signals (e.g., response latency), and LLM-generated embeddings of dialogue context. We will train the policy on historical human-LLM tutoring logs, using a reward function that balances a primary learning gain proxy (e.g., post-dialogue quiz score) against a penalty for interaction time, with safeguards against reward hacking via human feedback.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for optimizing the efficiency of LLM-mediated learning conversations, 2) empirical evidence on the time-effectiveness trade-offs for different conversational strategies, and 3) an open-source policy that can be integrated into educational LLM applications to reduce operational costs while maintaining quality.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally framing conversational learning efficiency as a bandit optimization problem with a composite reward function (learning + time), and in using the LLM's own state representations as context for a meta-decision maker that controls the LLM's conversational behavior.

proposals.title: Modeling Informational Niches: A Longitudinal Field Study of Casual Learning with Adaptive Chatbots
proposals.motivation: Understanding how to sustain long-term, curiosity-driven learning outside formal classrooms is a critical open problem in education and AI. Current educational technologies often lack models of how learning unfolds over time in diverse, real-world contexts, limiting their ability to provide personalized, long-term support. This gap hinders the design of effective, equitable learning tools for informal settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting ecological niche modeling techniques to longitudinal interaction data from educational chatbots, we can identify and computationally model the contextual and interactional 'informational niches' that correlate with sustained, self-directed learning progress.
proposals.method: We will deploy LLM-augmented chatbots as persistent 'sensors' in varied informal settings (e.g., libraries, homes) for a 12-month longitudinal study. We will collect fine-grained data on learner queries, engagement, and contextual variables. Using offline contextual bandit methods, we will model the reward (sustained engagement and topic depth) as a function of context and chatbot interventions. The resulting model will map the 'niche'—the high-dimensional space of conditions and supports—where casual learning thrives.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework for analyzing longitudinal learning pathways in the wild. It will also yield design principles for building educational chatbots that can diagnose and adaptively support a learner's unique informational niche, moving beyond short-term task completion to fostering long-term curiosity.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual and technical adaptation of ecological niche modeling—a method from conservation biology—to the domain of long-term human learning, framing the chatbot as an interactive sensor network and learning supports as environmental variables in a dynamic ecosystem.

proposals.title: Optimizing Pedagogical Dialogue Policies via Off-Policy Evaluation of Dynamic Treatment Regimes
proposals.motivation: LLM-powered dialogue tutors show promise but often use static pedagogical strategies, limiting their ability to personalize to a learner's dynamic cognitive and affective state. This is analogous to the challenge in precision medicine of tailoring treatments to a patient's evolving condition. Adapting the framework of Dynamic Treatment Regimes (DTRs) from clinical decision support offers a principled, data-driven method to optimize sequences of conversational interventions for long-term learning outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling a learner's dialogue state (knowledge, confusion, engagement) and framing conversational strategies (e.g., direct feedback vs. Socratic hinting) as clinical-style interventions will allow us to learn a personalized dialogue policy that significantly improves pedagogical outcomes over a one-size-fits-all approach.
proposals.method: We will collect interaction logs from an LLM-based tutor (e.g., based on Bloom) where multiple dialogue strategies are randomly assigned. We will define a state representation from dialogue history and learner features. Using offline contextual bandit and DTR methods (e.g., fitted Q-iteration, doubly robust off-policy evaluation), we will learn and evaluate a policy mapping states to optimal strategies. The final policy will be deployed and tested in an A/B study.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for personalizing educational dialogue agents, grounded in causal inference and DTRs. It will yield a concrete, optimized dialogue policy and empirical evidence on the impact of adaptive pedagogical strategies, advancing the field beyond content sequencing to interaction-style optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical translation of the Dynamic Treatment Regime framework from clinical medicine to conversational AI for education, moving beyond reward function repair to optimizing sequences of interactive pedagogical 'treatments' based on rich learner state.

proposals.title: A Controlled Trial Framework for Interactive Learning: Comparing Dialogue, Games, and Simulations via Multi-Armed Bandits and LLM Feedback
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of interactive learning technologies (e.g., dialogue tutors, educational games, simulations) has outpaced rigorous, standardized evaluation. Current research often provides simplistic, one-dimensional comparisons, failing to inform educators and policymakers about which modality is optimal for specific contexts and learner profiles. This creates inefficiency in resource allocation and suboptimal learning outcomes, mirroring the pre-clinical trial era of medicine where treatments were adopted without systematic efficacy mapping.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, multi-outcome evaluation framework, adapted from clinical trials, will reveal significant interactions between learning modalities (dialogue, games, simulations) and learner characteristics (prior knowledge, cognitive style), enabling the creation of a predictive model for optimal modality assignment that outperforms a one-size-fits-all approach.
proposals.method: We will design an online study where participants learn a standardized topic (e.g., introductory probability) via one of three randomly assigned, LLM-augmented modalities: a dialogue-based tutor (using a fine-tuned LLM for Socratic feedback), a serious game, or a simulation. We will employ an offline contextual multi-armed bandit algorithm to analyze the collected data, treating the modality as the 'arm' and learner traits as context, to efficiently estimate heterogeneous treatment effects. Primary outcomes (post-test scores, near-transfer tasks) and secondary outcomes (engagement surveys, interaction logs) will be measured. The reward function for the bandit analysis will be a composite of these outcomes, with potential refinement using human-in-the-loop feedback to mitigate reward misspecification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, standardized protocol for comparative efficacy research in educational technology, 2) empirical evidence on the conditional efficacy of major interactive learning formats, and 3) a data-driven policy for matching learners to optimal modalities, advancing beyond blanket recommendations.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of clinical trial rigor and causal inference methods (contextual bandits) to learning format comparison, and in its integration of LLMs for scalable interaction and human feedback for robust reward shaping, moving beyond single-metric A/B testing.

proposals.title: Assist-as-Needed Pedagogical Scaffolding: A Bandit-Based Framework for Minimally Invasive Tutoring
proposals.motivation: Intelligent tutoring systems (ITS) often struggle with providing the right level of support, risking learner disengagement through over-assistance or frustration through under-assistance. This problem mirrors the 'slacking' issue in rehabilitation robotics, where excessive robotic support impedes patient recovery. Adapting the successful 'assist-as-needed' (AAN) control paradigm from robotics to education offers a principled way to optimize scaffolding interventions, a core challenge in educational technology and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: An ITS that dynamically triggers scaffolding interventions based on a real-time, uncertainty-aware estimate of learner 'need'—inspired by AAN controllers—will improve learning efficiency and long-term skill retention compared to static or threshold-based scaffolding policies, by promoting more active problem-solving.
proposals.method: We will formalize learner cognitive state as a latent variable, estimated via an LLM-based analysis of dialogue and action sequences (akin to Bloom). The 'need' for assistance is defined as the deviation from an expected solution trajectory, quantified by prediction uncertainty. A contextual multi-armed bandit (CMAB) framework, building on Brunskill's prior work, will serve as the AAN controller, where the 'assist' action is only taken when the estimated need (context) exceeds a dynamically learned threshold. The bandit's reward function will be shaped by immediate correctness and long-term practice performance, with mechanisms to mitigate reward hacking.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, formal framework for adaptive scaffolding grounded in control theory, providing a clear operational definition of 'need'. It will yield a new bandit algorithm for educational interventions that explicitly minimizes unnecessary assistance, and empirical evidence on how such a policy affects learning behaviors and outcomes in simulated and real-world tutoring environments.
proposals.novelty: While CMABs have been used for intervention timing, the explicit analogy to AAN control—with its focus on minimal support and active learning promotion—and the integration of LLMs for real-time cognitive state estimation to define the 'assist' context is a novel synthesis of robotics, machine learning, and learning science principles.

proposals.title: RobustTutor: An Adversarially Robust Model-Tracing Tutor that Detects and Adapts to Security Attacks
proposals.motivation: The deployment of LLM-augmented Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS) introduces critical security vulnerabilities, including data poisoning and prompt injection, which can corrupt pedagogical policies and student models. These risks undermine the trustworthiness and safety of AI-driven education, especially as systems like Bloom explore behavior change. A secure ITS must be robust to such attacks while maintaining effective, personalized instruction.
proposals.hypothesis: An ITS that integrates adversarial input detection into its student modeling and tutorial action selection can maintain pedagogical efficacy under attack and can use detected anomalies as opportunities for teaching AI security concepts.
proposals.method: We will extend a model-tracing ITS core with a detection module trained via offline contextual bandits on historical student interaction logs, flagged with simulated adversarial patterns (e.g., anomalous solution steps, semantic outliers). The tutorial policy, a function mapping student state to feedback, will be equipped with a robust MDP formulation where certain states trigger security-adaptive dialogue moves. We will evaluate using human participants in a programming tutor, measuring learning gains and the system's performance in identifying injected attacks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for adversarially robust ITS design, 2) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between security robustness and pedagogical reward, and 3) a prototype system that demonstrates how to repair educational policy functions compromised by reward hacking attempts.
proposals.novelty: The synergy lies in repurposing the ITS's core student modeling for real-time security anomaly detection and framing adversarial interactions as teachable moments, a dual-purpose approach not explored in prior secure ML or educational technology work.

proposals.title: Scaffolding LLM-Based Tutoring with Model-Tracing and Policy-Guided Feedback
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) show promise as dialogue-based tutors but often lack the structured pedagogical scaffolding of proven Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS). ITS techniques like model-tracing and cognitive tutors provide granular, adaptive support by comparing student actions to a model of correct knowledge. Integrating these methods could address key limitations in LLM tutoring, such as inconsistent feedback and reward misalignment, to improve learning outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting an LLM-based dialogue tutor with a model-tracing cognitive engine and a policy for selecting feedback interventions will yield more effective and pedagogically sound scaffolding, leading to better student performance and engagement compared to standard LLM prompting.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-component system. First, a model-tracing engine, built on a pre-2026 cognitive model (e.g., ACT-R or a production rule set), will parse student problem-solving steps within a constrained domain (e.g., introductory programming). Second, an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) will be fine-tuned to generate natural language explanations, hints, and questions, conditioned on the model-tracer's assessment of the student's knowledge state. A contextual multi-armed bandit policy, informed by prior work on customizing feedback, will select from a set of pre-defined, LLM-augmented intervention types to optimize a reward function based on learning gains and engagement, with safeguards against reward hacking.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel hybrid architecture that combines the structured reasoning of ITS with the flexible dialogue of LLMs. It will provide empirical evidence on how to ground LLM pedagogical decisions in cognitive models and demonstrate a policy-based method for managing feedback interventions that balances immediate help with long-term learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for open-ended dialogue or bandits for feedback selection, the integration of a formal model-tracing engine to directly steer LLM-generated scaffolding and manage reward signals is a novel synthesis of classic AI in education and modern LLM techniques.

proposals.title: Incorporating Teacher Priors and Partial Models for Sample-Efficient Policy Learning in Educational Domains
proposals.motivation: In domains like education, deploying reinforcement learning (RL) from scratch is often infeasible due to sample inefficiency and safety concerns. However, practitioners (e.g., teachers, curriculum designers) possess valuable prior knowledge about task structure, student learning trajectories, or effective intervention types. Current meta-RL and contextual bandit methods often assume only shared structure across tasks, failing to incorporate this rich, heterogeneous side information, limiting personalization and speed of adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formally incorporating practitioner-provided partial models or side information as priors over the task distribution will significantly improve the sample efficiency and safety of policy learning algorithms in interactive settings like intelligent tutoring systems.
proposals.method: We will extend offline contextual bandit and meta-RL frameworks (e.g., PEARL) to accept probabilistic priors over task parameters (e.g., reward function, transition dynamics). For bandits, we will develop a Bayesian hierarchical model where teacher-provided features (e.g., student attributes, lesson difficulty) shape the prior for a student's reward model. For policy learning, we will adapt model-based RL to use a learned world model initialized with a simulator informed by domain theory. We will evaluate using simulated educational environments (e.g., ASSISTments) and a human-in-the-loop study where educators provide structured feedback to shape the prior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for integrating human domain knowledge into sequential decision-making algorithms, moving beyond shared structure to leverage heterogeneous side information. It will yield new algorithms with provable sample complexity improvements and demonstrate their practical value in enabling safer, more personalized educational interventions that adapt quickly from limited student interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses human feedback to shape rewards (e.g., 'Repairing Reward Functions'), our approach directly incorporates partial structural knowledge about the task distribution itself. This goes beyond standard meta-learning assumptions and explicitly bridges LLM-augmented knowledge elicitation (as in 'Bloom') with robust, sample-efficient policy optimization.

proposals.title: Mitigating Reward Hacking in Non-Stationary Multi-Task Learning via Human-in-the-Loop Feedback
proposals.motivation: Real-world RL deployments, such as educational tools that adapt to shifting student needs or policies for dynamic populations, face non-stationary task distributions. This drift can cause learned policies to exploit outdated reward functions, leading to reward hacking and degraded performance. Pre-2026, methods for repairing reward functions or customizing feedback assume relatively stationary environments, leaving a critical gap for sequential, evolving task settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning approach, which uses periodic human feedback to detect and correct for distributional shift, can maintain policy robustness and mitigate reward hacking in non-stationary multi-task RL more effectively than static reward functions or scheduled retraining.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a meta-RL problem with a latent, drifting task distribution. The core algorithm will be an extension of Contextual Multi-Armed Bandits and gradient-based meta-learning (e.g., MAML), augmented with a feedback module inspired by 'Repairing Reward Functions with Feedback'. At scheduled intervals, a human (e.g., a teacher/supervisor) provides binary or scalar feedback on recent policy behavior. This feedback will be used to train a shift detector and to generate a corrective reward signal, which fine-tunes the meta-policy's inner-loop adaptation process, preventing over-optimization to obsolete task modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework and algorithm for continual multi-task RL under non-stationarity, explicitly integrating human feedback for online reward correction. It will provide empirical benchmarks in simulated educational and policy domains, demonstrating how to sustain long-term utility when the world changes. The results will advance the design of reliable, human-aligned adaptive systems.
proposals.novelty: While non-stationary RL and reward hacking are studied separately, their intersection is less explored. The novelty lies in combining meta-learning for task structure with explicit, intermittent human feedback loops to dynamically 'repair' the learning objective itself in response to drift, rather than just adapting the policy.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Task Inference in Adaptive Educational Systems: Fast Adaptation from Limited Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: In educational technology, systems must adapt to diverse student needs and learning tasks, but collecting extensive, personalized interaction data for each new student or concept is impractical. Pre-2026, while LLMs offer rich feedback and bandits optimize discrete choices, there is a gap in rapidly inferring the underlying learning task from minimal, contextually rich human feedback to enable sample-efficient personalization. This problem is critical for scaling effective, real-time educational interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework, pre-trained on a distribution of simulated and historical educational interaction tasks, can rapidly infer a new student's or educator's underlying task model from a small amount of feedback, enabling more effective subsequent policy adaptation than non-meta-learned baselines.
proposals.method: We will employ a MAML-based architecture where the inner-loop adaptation uses a few steps of gradient descent on a contextually rich feedback signal (e.g., textual feedback from an LLM-augmented interface or binary correctness labels). The 'task' is parameterized by a student's latent learning style and the target concept. The outer loop meta-trains the model's initial parameters on a diverse distribution of such tasks constructed from existing educational interaction datasets. We will evaluate adaptation speed and final performance on held-out simulated students and via a human-subject study with an online tutoring interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel meta-learning method for task inference specifically designed for human-in-the-loop, feedback-rich domains like education. It will provide empirical evidence on the sample efficiency gains of meta-learning for adapting AI-driven educational tools, advancing towards more data-efficient and responsive personalized learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating MAML for explicit task inference from the specific, structured feedback types common in education (e.g., hints, corrections) and connecting this to the policy optimization problem for intervention selection, bridging meta-learning with the researcher's prior work on feedback and bandits in educational contexts.

proposals.title: Online Convex Optimization for Adaptive LLM Reward Repair with Human-in-the-Loop Feedback
proposals.motivation: LLMs deployed for interactive tasks, such as educational tutoring or behavior change, often suffer from reward hacking or misalignment when fine-tuned on static reward functions. Pre-2026 work, including 'Repairing Reward Functions with Feedback,' shows the need for iterative reward correction but relies on offline or batch updates, which are slow to adapt to sequential user feedback. An online learning framework is needed to efficiently and robustly adapt model behavior from a continuous stream of human feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing the sequential adaptation of an LLM's reward function as an online convex optimization (OCO) problem will enable more sample-efficient and stable policy updates from human feedback, reducing reward hacking and improving task performance compared to offline batch retraining methods.
proposals.method: We will model the LLM's reward function parameters as the decision set in an OCO framework. In each interaction round, the model receives a convex loss defined by human feedback (e.g., pairwise comparisons, scalar ratings, or textual corrections) on its generated outputs. We will implement and evaluate algorithms like Online Gradient Descent and Follow-The-Regularized-Leader (FTRL) for parameter updates. The system will be tested in a simulated educational dialogue environment, where the 'reward' is alignment with pedagogical goals, and feedback is provided by simulated students or educators.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled online learning algorithm for real-time LLM reward alignment, directly extending offline reward repair methods. It will provide theoretical regret bounds on performance and empirical evidence of improved adaptation speed and robustness in interactive AI-assisted learning scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While OCO is well-studied, its application to dynamically repairing LLM reward functions from sequential human feedback is novel, bridging the gap between theoretical online optimization and practical, safe LLM deployment in human-centric domains like education.

proposals.title: PAC-Certified Policy Learning: Guaranteeing Performance for Supervised Learning Policies in Education and Behavior Change
proposals.motivation: In high-stakes domains like education and behavior change, policies derived from supervised learning (e.g., from logged data or LLM-generated feedback) lack formal guarantees on their future performance. This is a critical barrier to safe deployment, as seen in prior work on mitigating reward hacking and customizing feedback. PAC learning theory offers a framework to provide such guarantees, but its application to complex, human-in-the-loop policy learning remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing policy learning from observational or preference feedback as a supervised learning problem within a PAC framework, we can derive sample complexity bounds and performance certificates that guarantee, with high probability, that the learned policy will perform nearly as well as a target policy on the true underlying population.
proposals.method: We will formalize the problem: given a dataset of state-action-reward tuples (or preference feedback), we treat policy learning as learning a classifier mapping states to actions. We will adapt PAC analysis for agnostic learning, using the empirical risk minimization principle. The core technical step involves deriving uniform convergence bounds for the policy class (e.g., neural networks or LLM-generated feedback rules), accounting for the structure of the reward function and potential distribution shift. We will implement an algorithm that, given a dataset and a policy class, outputs a learned policy and a certified performance gap relative to the best-in-class policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded methodology for certifying the quality of policies learned from offline data or human feedback, directly addressing safety concerns in reward learning. It will yield practical tools for educators and system designers to assess the reliability of data-driven intervention policies before deployment.
proposals.novelty: While PAC theory is classic, its direct application to certify policies in interactive, human-centric domains like education—bridging the gap between theoretical learning theory and applied policy learning for LLM-augmented systems—is novel. It moves beyond empirical validation to provide provable assurances.

proposals.title: Policy-Aware Off-Policy Evaluation for Non-Stationary Behavior Policies in Adaptive Educational Systems
proposals.motivation: Off-policy evaluation (OPE) is crucial for safely assessing new educational interventions using historical data. However, in real-world adaptive systems like intelligent tutoring platforms, the behavior policy—the algorithm determining feedback or hints—continuously updates based on student performance. Standard OPE estimators assume a stationary behavior policy, leading to biased value estimates when this assumption is violated, which can misguide policy improvement and harm student learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the temporal evolution of the behavior policy within an OPE framework will yield lower mean squared error in value estimation compared to stationary baselines, enabling more reliable policy optimization in non-stationary educational environments.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel estimator that integrates a model of the behavior policy's adaptation mechanism. Building on the Doubly Robust estimator, we will incorporate per-trajectory or time-window-specific importance weights, tracking policy changes logged in system metadata. For settings with high-dimensional contexts (e.g., LLM-augmented tutors), we will use recurrent neural networks or transformer encoders to model the policy sequence. We will validate the method using logged data from adaptive educational tools and a simulated benchmark where a bandit-based tutor policy evolves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled OPE method tailored for non-stationary behavior policies in education technology. It will deliver a practical toolkit for researchers and practitioners to accurately evaluate new teaching policies from historical logs of evolving systems, reducing the risk of deploying suboptimal interventions. The work bridges the gap between offline RL theory and the operational reality of continuously improving educational AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work addresses non-stationarity in rewards or environments, our focus on explicitly modeling the non-stationary behavior policy sequence for OPE is novel. This directly addresses a core, often-overlooked challenge in deploying and evaluating adaptive learning systems and LLM-augmented behavior change tools that learn in production.

proposals.title: Micro-Scale Off-Policy Evaluation for Safe Online Learning in High-Stakes Educational Domains
proposals.motivation: Online reinforcement learning (RL) for personalized education, such as adaptive feedback systems, requires safe exploration to avoid harmful policies. Pre-2026, deploying such systems is risky because traditional off-policy evaluation (OPE) is designed for large, final evaluations, not for frequent, low-cost safety checks during learning. This creates a barrier to using online RL in real-world educational settings where student outcomes are sensitive.
proposals.hypothesis: That a novel, micro-scale OPE framework, using high-confidence off-policy estimators on small, recent data batches, can reliably detect performance degradation of an online learning policy early enough to guide safe exploration without excessive regret.
proposals.method: We will develop a micro-scale OPE protocol that integrates with an online contextual bandit learner (e.g., a neural LinUCB). At frequent intervals, we will compute a high-confidence lower bound (e.g., using a concentration inequality like Hoeffding's or a bootstrap method) on the performance of the current policy using a small, recent replay buffer. This bound will be compared to a safety threshold derived from a baseline or previous policy. If the bound falls below the threshold, exploration is constrained, and the policy is rolled back or adjusted. We will test this using logged data from educational technology platforms and in simulated student interaction environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for integrating frequent, lightweight safety checks into online learning systems, reducing the risk of deploying RL in education. It will advance the field of safe RL by bridging the gap between offline evaluation and online adaptation, and deliver a practical toolkit for educational policy optimization that respects the high-stakes nature of student learning.
proposals.novelty: While OPE is well-studied, its application for frequent, micro-scale safety monitoring during *online* learning is novel. This approach uniquely combines high-confidence bounds with a rollback mechanism to create a 'circuit breaker' for safe exploration, distinct from prior work on pure offline evaluation or conservative exploration without frequent OPE checks.

proposals.title: Adaptive Regularization for Robust Reward Learning from Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Algorithms that learn reward functions from human feedback are prone to reward hacking, where they exploit misspecifications to achieve high proxy reward at the expense of true objectives. This is a critical safety and alignment problem in real-world applications like education and behavior change, where feedback is sparse and non-stationary. Pre-2026 online learning theory offers strong regret guarantees but is not fully leveraged for designing robust, adaptive reward learning procedures.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing reward learning from iterative human feedback within an online adaptive regularization or Follow-the-Regularized-Leader (FTRL) framework will yield algorithms with provable regret bounds against reward hacking, while empirically improving robustness and sample efficiency in interactive settings like educational tools.
proposals.method: We will develop an online algorithm where, at each round, the agent proposes a policy, receives human feedback (e.g., comparative evaluations or corrections), and updates a reward function parameterization (e.g., a neural network or linear function). The core innovation is the design of a data-dependent adaptive regularizer within an FTRL update, informed by the history of feedback and policy performance. This regularizer will penalize large shifts in the reward function that could lead to hacking, adapting its strength based on the inferred uncertainty in the feedback. We will integrate this with LLM-based feedback interpretation modules from prior work (e.g., 'Bloom') to handle natural language.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new theoretically-grounded algorithm for robust reward learning with a regret bound formalizing protection against reward hacking, and 2) empirical validation in simulated and human-subject studies involving educational feedback scenarios, demonstrating improved alignment and sample efficiency over existing baseline methods.
proposals.novelty: While FTRL is known in online learning, its application to design adaptive regularizers that explicitly mitigate reward hacking in human-in-the-loop reward learning is novel. This bridges the theoretical guarantees of online convex optimization with the practical challenges of reward misspecification in human-AI interaction.

proposals.title: Adaptive Per-Step Feedback via Contextual Bandits for LLM-Augmented Tutoring Systems
proposals.motivation: LLM-augmented tutoring systems, like those explored in 'Bloom', provide a powerful platform for behavior change and learning, but delivering effective feedback at each step of a student's interaction remains a core challenge. Current methods often use static or heuristic rules, which fail to adapt to the student's evolving context and may lead to suboptimal learning outcomes or disengagement. This problem is critical for scaling personalized education and aligns with the need for data-driven, adaptive policy design in human-AI interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the per-time-step feedback selection problem within an LLM-augmented tutor as a contextual bandit will yield more effective and personalized learning interventions than static feedback policies, as measured by student performance and engagement metrics.
proposals.method: We will frame each decision point (e.g., type of hint, level of encouragement) within a tutoring session as an arm in a contextual bandit. Building on prior work on offline bandits for education, we will use an algorithm like LinUCB or a Thompson sampling variant to select actions. The context will include real-time features from the student's interaction history, the current problem state, and inferred student affect from the LLM's analysis. We will evaluate the approach using logged interaction data and via an A/B test in a simulated or controlled real-world tutoring environment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, theoretically grounded framework for adaptive, per-step decision-making in interactive AI tutoring systems. It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of bandit-based feedback policies compared to baselines, advancing the field of AI in education towards more responsive and personalized support. The resulting policy and analysis will offer concrete design principles for LLM-augmented behavior change systems.
proposals.novelty: While bandits have been used for coarse-grained intervention scheduling in education, applying them for fine-grained, per-step action selection within the dynamic, stateful context of an LLM-driven tutoring conversation is a novel synthesis. This bridges stochastic bandit theory with the real-time adaptation challenges of modern generative AI interfaces for human learning.

ideas.title: Evaluating Engagement and Learning via Complex, Non-Dialogic Interaction Tasks
ideas.description: This research develops a suite of highly complex, multi-step interactive tasks (e.g., constructing a causal model from evidence, debugging a flawed simulation) that require deep user engagement but are explicitly non-conversational. These tasks are deployed to evaluate both user learning outcomes/engagement and the AI's capability to scaffold such complex interactions without dialogue. The synergy lies in using scaled evaluation complexity not just to benchmark model performance, but as an experimental tool to dissect the mechanisms of human-AI engagement, potentially revealing that sophisticated joint reasoning, not dialogue per se, is the core driver of benefit.

ideas.title: Distributed, Personalized Cognitive Tutors: Federated Learning of ITS-Scaffolded Dialogue Agents
ideas.description: This research direction develops algorithms to train large-scale intelligent tutoring systems (ITS) as dialogue agents across a federated network of smaller, geographically dispersed compute clusters, such as those in schools, universities, or regional data centers. Each local cluster trains on its private, siloed student interaction data, using the ITS techniques (e.g., model-tracing, knowledge component modeling) to structure the learning task and generate pedagogical scaffolding. The core synergy is that the federated training process not only builds a general pedagogical model but also enables the emergent, privacy-preserving discovery of globally effective tutoring strategies and common misconception pathways, which are then encoded into the agent's adaptive scaffolding rules, creating a tutor that improves collectively from diverse, real-world classrooms without centralizing sensitive data.

ideas.title: Personalized Dialogue Training via Micro-Domain Quality Corpora
ideas.description: This research proposes training dialogue models for personalized tutoring not on massive, generic web text, but on small, meticulously curated 'micro-corpora' of successful human tutoring sessions. Each corpus is defined by a specific pedagogical strategy (e.g., Socratic questioning, worked examples, motivational feedback) and annotated with learner profiles and outcomes. The model learns to map from a learner's ongoing interaction pattern to the most effective conversational strategy, adapting its dialogue style in real-time. This enables deep personalization of *how* the system teaches, grounded in proven, high-quality instructional interactions rather than statistically common patterns from the web.

ideas.title: Apply intelligent tutoring system scaffolding

ideas.sourceTitle: QuizBot: A Dialogue-based Adaptive Learning System for Factual Knowledge

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Personalization for Sustained Casual Learning in AI Systems
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel application of control theory to design AI systems that actively optimize for long-term user adoption in casual learning contexts. The core idea is to treat a user's sustained engagement—predicted by their stated and inferred casual learning preferences—as a primary control objective for model alignment. The system would employ formal feedback mechanisms to monitor interaction patterns and dynamically adjust parameters like task granularity, explanation depth, and reinforcement schedules to stabilize the user's learning trajectory. This moves beyond static personalization, creating a self-correcting AI that formally aligns its outputs with the behavioral outcome of long-term habitual use.

ideas.title: Decentralized, Auditable Cognitive Tutors: Open Frameworks for Pedagogical Model Development
ideas.description: This research proposes a decentralized, open-source framework specifically for developing, composing, and auditing the core pedagogical components of intelligent tutoring systems (ITS). Instead of a single, opaque 'tutor model,' the framework enables a community to build, share, and critique modular 'cognitive model' and 'tutorial strategy' components that govern a dialogue agent's teaching interactions. This allows for independent verification of pedagogical claims, collaborative improvement of tutoring tactics, and the creation of a diverse, auditable marketplace of teaching strategies that can be tailored to different domains and learner needs, fundamentally decentralizing control over how AI teaches.

ideas.title: Participatory design with online learner communities
ideas.description: Use co-design methods from human-computer interaction to develop interventions with students.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling up behavioral science interventions in online education

ideas.title: Optimizing for Knowledge Utility: Interleaving Recall Practice with Application Trials for Robust Factual Learning
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that factual knowledge is most robustly learned through a protocol that deliberately interleaves pure recall practice with application trials. After an initial learning phase, learners engage in cycles where recalling a fact is immediately followed by using that fact to make an inference, solve a novel micro-problem, or integrate it with prior knowledge. The system measures long-term retention, speed of access, and—critically—transfer of the factual knowledge to new contexts. The goal is to identify the optimal balance and sequencing of recall and application that moves learning beyond inert memorization toward flexible, usable knowledge.

ideas.title: Dynamic investigations of contextual heterogeneity
ideas.description: Design and test adaptive interventions that change based on student and course context to improve efficacy.

ideas.title: Adaptive Dialogue Strategy Optimization via a Clinical Treatment Regime Framework
ideas.description: This research proposes to personalize dialogue systems beyond content sequencing by adapting the solution to a parallel problem in precision medicine: optimizing dynamic treatment regimes (DTRs). In clinical decision support, DTRs use patient data to recommend personalized treatment actions over time to improve long-term outcomes. The analogous solution involves learning decision rules from data that map a patient's evolving state to the most effective intervention. Adapted to conversational AI, this involves modeling the learner's state within a dialogue (incorporating factors like knowledge gaps, affective state, and interaction history) and formulating adaptive conversational strategies (e.g., feedback directness, hint granularity, motivational tone) as 'treatments.' The core research idea is to develop and optimize a dialogue strategy regime—a data-driven policy that selects the most effective conversational action for a given learner state at each turn, with the objective of maximizing pedagogical outcomes such as comprehension and retention, thereby creating a dialogue agent that dynamically tailors its interactive style to the individual.

ideas.title: Leverage online learning reduction techniques

ideas.title: Interaction Quality vs. Duration: Disentangling the Mediators Between Chatbot Engagement and Learning Outcomes
ideas.description: This study investigates not just if more time with a chatbot leads to learning, but how the specific qualities of that interaction determine its effectiveness. It employs detailed interaction analytics to categorize behaviors (e.g., rehearsing, elaborating, debugging misconceptions) and links these patterns to pre-post assessments of knowledge and transfer. The design explicitly tests whether time spent is a reliable proxy for learning or if its effect is fully mediated by the depth and correctness of the cognitive processes engaged during the interaction.

ideas.title: Mapping the Failure Modes of Conversational Transfer for Educational Engagement
ideas.description: This research investigates the intersection of transfer learning failures and educational engagement by hypothesizing that the performance degradation of adapted foundation models is most acute in pedagogically complex tasks (e.g., conceptual reasoning, debugging misconceptions) where a full conversational interface provides unique scaffolding. It will systematically test if simpler, non-conversational interactions (e.g., multiple-choice, static feedback) are sufficient for engagement only in tasks where the model transfers robustly, thereby creating a predictive map linking transfer failure domains to the necessity of dialogue. The outcome is a principled guide for when the cost of building a full chatbot is justified by both model capability gaps and genuine learning needs.

ideas.title: Assessing and Scaffolding Transferable Conceptual Models through Adversarial Dialogue
ideas.description: This research investigates whether dialogue-based agents can not only deliver conceptual explanations but also diagnose and repair gaps in a learner's mental model to ensure robust, transferable understanding. The system engages students in a core teaching dialogue, then interleaves 'adversarial assessment probes'—novel mini-scenarios or counterfactuals that test the ability to apply the concept under new constraints. By analyzing the student's reasoning path in response to these probes, the system identifies specific weaknesses (e.g., over-generalization, missing causal links) and tailors subsequent dialogue to confront and correct those misconceptions, aiming for understanding that generalizes beyond the training examples.

ideas.title: Efficiency-Aware Transfer Learning for Conversational Pedagogy
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to optimize conversational learning efficiency by first systematically identifying the specific pedagogical tasks, learner profiles, and knowledge domains where standard foundation model adaptation (e.g., via fine-tuning or prompt engineering) fails or degrades performance. It then uses this map of transfer learning pitfalls to guide the creation of specialized, efficient interaction protocols—such as strategic prompting, constrained dialogue, or hybrid model routing—that bypass ineffective adaptations. The result is a new class of conversational learning systems that achieve maximal time efficiency not through generic compression, but by intelligently avoiding the known failure modes of transferred knowledge, ensuring robust learning outcomes.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Intelligent Tutoring Systems for Secure and Trustworthy AI Pedagogy
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the integration of adversarial security principles into the core scaffolding mechanisms of an Intelligent Tutoring System (ITS). The goal is to develop a dialogue-based pedagogical agent that employs model-tracing and cognitive tutoring techniques while being robust to data poisoning, prompt injection, and model extraction attacks that could corrupt its teaching or leak its proprietary instructional model. The novel synergy lies in using the ITS's student modeling capability to detect anomalous input patterns indicative of attacks, adapting its tutorial dialogue to counter or investigate these attempts, and potentially using these incidents as teachable moments for learners about AI security. This results in a dual-purpose system that provides secure, reliable instruction and inherently educates about the vulnerabilities of the foundation models it is built upon.

ideas.title: Comparative Efficacy Framework for Interactive Learning Modalities
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the comparative efficacy framework from pharmacology to the evaluation of interactive learning formats. Just as new drugs are tested against standard treatments in controlled trials measuring multiple outcome dimensions, this study would create a standardized protocol to compare dialogue-based learning, educational games, and simulations. The framework would measure primary outcomes (knowledge retention, skill transfer), secondary outcomes (engagement, motivation, cognitive load), and moderating variables (learner prior knowledge, cognitive style, age) to determine not just which format is 'better,' but which is optimally suited for specific educational contexts and learning goals, moving beyond simplistic comparisons to a nuanced, evidence-based typology of interactive learning efficacy.

ideas.title: Decomposing Dialogue: Testing the 'Active Ingredient' in Interactive Learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving approach of pharmacological fractionation to challenge the assumption that full dialogue is necessary for engagement gains. In pharmacology, complex compounds are broken down to isolate their 'active ingredient.' The analogous solution is to apply this method of systematic decomposition to educational interactivity. The adapted research idea is to dissect a full-chatbot's functionality into distinct, non-conversational interactive components (e.g., structured choice points, reactive feedback, scaffolded input). By testing these simplified components individually and in combination against the full dialogue system and a static control, the study aims to identify the specific type and level of interactivity that constitutes the minimal sufficient 'active ingredient' for measurable benefits in engagement and learning.

ideas.title: Apply Rehabilitation Robotics' Assist-as-Needed Control to ITS Scaffolding
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'assist-as-needed' (AAN) control paradigm from rehabilitation robotics to intelligent tutoring system (ITS) scaffolding. In robotic therapy, AAN controllers provide physical support precisely when a patient's movement deviates from a target trajectory, promoting active recovery. Analogously, the adapted pedagogical agent would use real-time analysis of learner dialogue and actions to estimate their cognitive state and 'need'. Scaffolding (e.g., hints, simplified problems) would be triggered dynamically only when the learner's predicted solution path shows significant deviation or impasse, with the system aiming to provide the minimal support necessary for progress. This creates a responsive, learner-adaptive support system that maximizes engagement and deep learning by avoiding over-assistance, similar to how robotic therapy avoids 'slacking' by the patient's neuromuscular system.

ideas.title: Auditing and Mitigating Algorithmic Bias in Personalized Educational Dialogue Systems
ideas.description: This research investigates the societal risks of inequity and misuse specifically within the domain of personalized educational dialogue agents. It develops interdisciplinary methods to audit how adaptive conversational strategies—tailored to individual learner needs—can inadvertently encode or amplify biases related to culture, language, socioeconomic status, or learning differences. The work will produce both ethical-legal frameworks and technical toolkits to detect when personalization algorithms create disparate educational outcomes or enable manipulative pedagogical practices. The goal is to engineer next-generation dialogue systems that are not only adaptive but also auditable and equitable by design.

ideas.title: Analyze non-stationary task distributions

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map and Support Casual Learning Pathways
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of ecological niche modeling from conservation biology to the problem of studying long-term casual learning with educational chatbots. In ecology, scientists deploy long-term sensor networks and citizen science tools to gather field data on species presence and environmental variables, which is used to model the ecological niches that support species survival. Analogously, this study proposes deploying educational chatbots as persistent, interactive 'sensors' in varied informal learning settings (e.g., public libraries, after-school clubs, home kitchens) to conduct longitudinal field studies. The chatbots will passively and actively collect fine-grained data on learner queries, engagement duration, topic evolution, and contextual factors over months or years. This data will then be used to computationally model the 'informational niche' or set of conditions—such as specific knowledge scaffolding, timely prompts, or social reinforcement—that correlate with sustained, curiosity-driven learning. The outcome is a framework for understanding casual learning ecosystems and for engineering adaptive chatbots that can identify and nurture a learner's unique pathway, much as conservation strategies are designed to support a species in its habitat.

ideas.title: Factual knowledge as muscle memory: Challenging recall's primacy with variable practice
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that factual knowledge is best learned via repeated recall (Q&A). Drawing an analogy from motor skill acquisition in sports science, where constant practice is inferior to variable practice for long-term learning, we adapt this solution. In motor learning, practicing a skill in varied contexts (variable practice) creates a more robust and adaptable memory than simple repetition. The adapted research idea proposes testing 'variable factual practice' against standard repeated recall. This would involve learners engaging with the same factual content through interleaved methods—such as generating explanations, elaborating with personal connections, and applying facts to novel scenarios—rather than blocked Q&A drills. The core hypothesis is that this variability, while potentially slowing initial performance, will lead to superior long-term retention and an ability to flexibly use the knowledge, challenging the presumed optimality of pure recall practice for factual learning.

ideas.title: Applying Habit Formation Theory from Psychology to Validate Casual Learning as a Sustained Engagement Driver
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a reported preference for casual learning ensures long-term adoption. Drawing an analogy from Health Psychology, where a preference for light exercise does not guarantee habitual activity, the solution involves moving beyond lab-based self-reports. The analogous field used longitudinal, real-world tracking (ecological momentary assessment) to link micro-behaviors to contextual cues and habit strength. Adapted to learning technology research, this proposes a longitudinal study deploying a learning app (e.g., for language or trivia) and using mixed methods—passive logging of micro-sessions and triggered in-the-moment surveys—to objectively measure if and how casual learning preferences translate into sustained use. It tests whether casual learning integrates into daily routines as a habit or dissipates post-study, identifying the key facilitators (e.g., notifications, embedded triggers) and barriers (e.g., cognitive load, context switching) to long-term adoption.

ideas.title: Neurally-Informed Cognitive Models for Optimizing Factual Learning
ideas.description: This research direction uses neuroscientific interpretability methods (like fMRI decoding or EEG representational similarity analysis) to directly observe how different learning strategies (e.g., recall, elaboration, generation) transform the neural representations of factual information in the human brain. By comparing these neural trajectories to the internal representational dynamics of AI models trained with analogous strategies, we can build computationally explicit models of the learning process. This allows us to test not just which method yields better behavioral recall, but why—by identifying the representational stability, differentiation, or integration patterns that correlate with long-term retention. The ultimate goal is to derive first-principles, neurally-grounded prescriptions for optimal factual learning protocols that are explainable at the level of evolving memory representations.

ideas.title: Auditing Temporal Learning Traces: Using Discovery Tools to Analyze How Extended Interaction Time Shapes Model Knowledge and Risk
ideas.description: This research applies the systematic, evidence-gathering approach of legal e-discovery to audit the *learning process* of a model during extended chatbot interactions. It investigates whether the extra time users spend with a model leads to the deep, structured integration of knowledge (including potentially copyrighted or sensitive concepts) or results in fragmented, inefficient recall. The synergy enables a novel audit trail that links specific user dialogue durations and patterns to the consolidation of knowledge in the model's parameters, providing a dynamic measure of legal and ethical risk that evolves with use, rather than a static snapshot of the training corpus.

ideas.title: Evaluating Factual Knowledge Architectures via Complex, Multi-Modal Reasoning Probes
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the need for complex evaluation with the study of knowledge acquisition methods. It proposes developing evaluation suites where core factual knowledge must be retrieved and applied within intricate, multi-step reasoning problems, narrative generation tasks, or counterfactual scenarios. This allows us to probe whether knowledge learned via simple recall fails under compositional pressure, and whether methods like elaboration lead to more robust and accessible knowledge structures. The outcome is a diagnostic tool that links training methodology directly to downstream performance on tasks requiring deep, reliable knowledge integration.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Driven Meta-Pre-training for Robust Domain Specialization
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid pre-training paradigm designed to overcome the brittleness of purely domain-specific models. The system uses a meta-learning framework where the model is trained on a curriculum of varied tasks, alternating between deep dives into a target domain and exposures to diverse, general corpora to maintain broad competencies. The objective is to learn how to efficiently specialize for a domain without catastrophically forgetting general knowledge, enabling strong in-domain performance while preserving robustness and adaptability. The outcome is a model that can be fine-tuned into a superior domain expert compared to models from either purely general or purely narrow pre-training.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Level Deconstruction: Comparing Core Engagement and Pedagogical Components Across Interactive Learning Formats
ideas.description: This research moves beyond superficial medium-to-medium comparisons by deconstructing dialogue systems, educational games, and simulations into their fundamental, replicable mechanisms for engagement (e.g., narrative, curiosity loops, aesthetic appeal) and pedagogy (e.g., feedback timing, scaffolding, conceptual embodiment). Using a controlled experimental framework, it isolates and directly tests these mechanisms across format boundaries. For example, it would compare the efficacy of a 'Socratic questioning' feedback mechanism when delivered via text dialogue, spoken dialogue, or as in-game hint text. The outcome is a transferable taxonomy of effective components, enabling principled design rather than medium-based guesswork.

ideas.title: Infer the task distribution from samples

ideas.sourceTitle: Sample Complexity of Multi-task Reinforcement Learning

ideas.title: Adversarial Resilience in Casual Learning Systems: A Longitudinal Security Framework
ideas.description: This research investigates how the security of foundation models against data poisoning and extraction attacks is impacted by, and must adapt to, the patterns of casual, long-term human use. It posits that adversarial vulnerabilities may evolve or be exploited differently when models are interacted with sporadically and non-expertly over extended periods, as opposed to in focused, short-term studies. The core question is whether security mechanisms that are robust in controlled settings degrade or cause friction that undermines sustained adoption in casual learning environments. The outcome aims to produce a new class of adversarial defenses that are not only technically robust but also aligned with human behavioral patterns to ensure both security and long-term usability.

ideas.title: Benchmark-Driven Design of Domain-Specific Pre-Training Regimes
ideas.description: This research proposes to develop a new methodology where comprehensive failure mode benchmarks are not just evaluation tools, but the core engine for designing domain-specific pre-training. Instead of simply testing if a specialized model is better, we use systematic robustness evaluations to identify the precise knowledge gaps and failure modes of general models within a target domain. These identified weaknesses then directly guide the curation of pre-training data and the structuring of the training curriculum for a new, domain-specific model. The outcome is a principled, benchmark-driven pipeline for constructing specialized foundation models that are provably more robust and performant on critical domain-specific tasks where general models falter.

ideas.title: Adversarial, Self-Improving Evaluation Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research designs an evaluation paradigm where the test suite itself is an active, learning adversary. Instead of a fixed set of tasks, the system uses automated agents to perpetually generate novel, multi-step problems, deceptive reasoning puzzles, and edge-case scenarios tailored to exploit the latest model's perceived strengths. The model under evaluation is then probed against this constantly evolving challenge set, measuring not just performance but robustness and adaptability. This creates a co-evolutionary arms race between model capabilities and evaluation complexity, preventing benchmark overfitting and providing a truer measure of general, flexible intelligence.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Regulation of Conversational Dynamics for Optimal Learning
ideas.description: This research proposes designing chatbots as adaptive controllers within a human-AI learning loop. By modeling user interactions (e.g., query depth, response latency, repetition patterns) as a dynamic system, control-theoretic principles (like PID controllers or model predictive control) are applied to stabilize the dialogue towards states conducive to deep processing. The system would not just measure time spent but would use formal stability criteria to dynamically adjust its own prompting, scaffolding, and feedback to counteract inefficient interaction modes, thereby directly testing whether guided time investment improves learning outcomes versus unguided interaction.

ideas.title: Predictive Framework for Anticipating and Diagnosing Transfer Learning Failure Modes
ideas.description: This research develops a diagnostic framework that predicts when and why foundation model adaptation will fail, rather than just documenting past failures. It involves constructing a meta-dataset of adaptation outcomes across diverse tasks and models, then training a meta-model to identify the key task and model characteristics that signal high risk of degradation. The resulting system provides actionable guidance for practitioners to assess adaptation feasibility and for researchers to design more robust adaptation techniques, fundamentally shifting the approach from post-hoc analysis to proactive risk assessment.

ideas.title: Human-in-the-loop intervention protocols

ideas.title: Designing Casual, Personalized Foundation Model Interfaces to Decentralize Adoption and Mitigate Systemic Risk
ideas.description: This research investigates how to design human-AI interaction interfaces that make using a diverse ecosystem of smaller, specialized, or locally-run foundation models as cognitively effortless and engaging as using a single, dominant model like a centralized chatbot. It moves beyond studying casual learning preference to actively engineering it as a tool for risk mitigation. The core hypothesis is that if interacting with multiple models can be made to feel casual, personalized, and contextually seamless—perhaps through meta-interfaces, automated routing, or playful exploration—users will voluntarily sustain a diversified usage pattern long-term. This shifts the solution to homogenization risks from a top-down architectural mandate to a user-centered adoption strategy, creating a more resilient and adaptable AI ecosystem.

ideas.title: Factual knowledge is best learned via recall

ideas.title: Co-optimized Model Ecosystems with a Learnable Interaction Grammar
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply assembling diverse models by co-designing the specialists and the rules of their collaboration. A lightweight, shared 'Interaction Grammar' is learned simultaneously with the specialized models, defining efficient protocols for communication, task routing, and output integration. This grammar minimizes coordination overhead, ensures robust error handling, and allows the entire ecosystem to be trained end-to-end for specific performance objectives. The result is a collaborative system that is more than the sum of its parts, achieving both the benefits of specialization and the coherence of a unified model.

ideas.title: Adapting Signal Compression for Conversational Learning Efficiency
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Digital Signal Processing, where compression algorithms (like MP3) reduce file size by removing redundant or imperceptible data without sacrificing perceived quality, this research aims to optimize conversation efficiency for learning. The analogous solution involves identifying and filtering out perceptually less critical information to maximize transmission efficiency. Adapted to conversational learning, the research idea is to develop computational methods that analyze learning dialogues to identify and compress 'noise'—such as tangential talk, overly redundant explanations, or non-essential social padding—while algorithmically preserving the core conceptual content and key engagement signals. The outcome would be techniques to generate or guide compressed learning conversations that significantly reduce time cost while maintaining, or even enhancing, learning effectiveness and engagement by focusing on the most pedagogically salient exchanges.

ideas.title: Epidemiological Modeling of Factual Recall Strategies in LLM Training and Adaptation
ideas.description: This research investigates how the choice of factual knowledge training strategy (recall Q&A, elaboration, generation) acts as a key variable in the 'transmission dynamics' of errors within model adaptation pipelines. By applying epidemiological models (e.g., SIR, network diffusion), we can simulate how inaccuracies introduced or reinforced during a model's initial factual training propagate with different virulence and persistence when the model is fine-tuned or adapted for new tasks. This allows for the comparative 'epidemiological risk profiling' of training methodologies, identifying which strategies are most likely to lead to localized, containable errors versus systemic 'outbreaks' of misinformation in downstream model ecosystems. The goal is to prescribe training protocols that optimize for factual robustness not just in a base model, but across its entire potential adaptation lineage.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Alignment for Emergent Reasoning: Benchmarking Capability Transfer from Scale to Efficiency
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that the emergent reasoning capabilities observed in large language models can be instilled in smaller models through explicit architectural inductive biases, rather than solely through scale. We design a compact, modular architecture that learns to compose a library of reusable, algorithm-like primitives during pre-training, enforcing a bias toward structured computation. The system is then rigorously evaluated against a carefully constructed benchmark of multi-step reasoning tasks designed to probe for 'emergent' performance discontinuities. Success is quantitatively defined as the small model matching the capability profile—including generalization patterns and error types—of a scaled model that exhibits emergent behavior on the same benchmark, thereby decoupling capability from parameter count.

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Pre-training as Ecological Specialization
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of broad, general pre-training by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's specialist-generalist paradigm. In ecology, specialists evolve traits optimized for a specific, stable niche where they outperform generalists, who sacrifice peak performance for breadth and flexibility. The analogous solution involves constructing resource gradients to map the performance trade-offs. Adapted to ML research, this means pre-training a family of models along a continuum of domain specificity—from models trained only on biomedical text to those trained only on legal code, versus broadly trained models. Their downstream performance would then be evaluated across a corresponding gradient of tasks (e.g., medical QA, legal summarization, general trivia, code generation) to empirically chart the conditions (task-domain alignment, data stability) under which targeted, domain-specific pre-training yields superior performance, effectively mapping the 'fitness landscape' for model specialization.

ideas.description: This research develops a self-improving evaluation ecosystem that continuously probes foundation models for novel robustness failures. It combines a core, updatable failure taxonomy with automated red-team agents that generate new test scenarios by mutating known failures, synthesizing edge cases from deployment logs, and exploring adversarial directions. The system dynamically updates a living benchmark suite, providing real-time failure mode detection and causal analysis, ensuring evaluation remains relevant as models scale and are applied in new contexts.

ideas.title: High-Fidelity Data Synthesis via Controlled Crystallization
ideas.description: This idea applies the materials science principle of controlled crystallization to machine learning data strategy. In crystal growth, high-purity single crystals are produced through meticulous processes like the Czochralski method, where growth conditions are tightly controlled from a small seed to ensure atomic perfection, rather than by rapidly solidifying a large, impure bulk. Analogously, we propose a 'data crystallization' framework for AI training. The core method involves first curating a small, meticulously verified, high-quality 'seed' dataset. Then, instead of scaling by adding massive, uncontrolled web data, we 'grow' the dataset through controlled, rule-based, or generative synthesis that is constrained to preserve the logical consistency, factual accuracy, and stylistic purity of the seed. This process prioritizes the structural integrity and fidelity of the training data—scale is achieved through synthetic expansion that maintains quality, not through aggregation of noise. The goal is to train models on datasets that are large yet internally consistent and high-fidelity, mirroring how a perfect crystal is grown from a defined nucleus rather than mined from a heterogeneous, flawed rock.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control for LLMs with Joint Dynamics and Objective Inference
ideas.description: This approach frames LLM alignment as an adaptive control problem where both the model's response dynamics and the precise alignment objective are treated as partially observable and evolving. A dual estimation framework continuously infers a latent representation of the current 'system' (the LLM's behavior manifold) and the 'reference signal' (the desired objective, derived from human feedback) using techniques from nonlinear system identification. An adaptive controller, such as a neural network-based policy trained via reinforcement learning with stability constraints derived from Lyapunov theory, then acts to steer the model's outputs. This ensures robust stability guarantees even as the model is fine-tuned or the human-provided alignment target shifts over time.

ideas.description: This approach uses a meticulously curated, small 'seed' dataset to define quality, but applies it at scale. Instead of training the final model directly on the small set, the system employs it to train automated quality auditors—specialized models that predict data utility, factual integrity, and stylistic alignment. These auditors dynamically filter and reweight a vast, ever-refreshing web corpus, constructing a large, high-quality training set that balances diversity with excellence. The process is iterative, using the performance of the main model to identify failure domains and refine the auditors, ensuring the scaled dataset evolves to cover broad competencies without sacrificing quality.

ideas.title: A Falsifiable Prediction Engine for Emergent Capabilities in Scaling Models
ideas.description: This research program develops theoretical frameworks under a strict predictive testing paradigm. Candidate theories must generate specific, quantifiable predictions about the emergence of new capabilities (e.g., 'Chain-of-thought reasoning emerges at a cross-entropy loss of < X') before models of a given scale are trained. The core methodology involves iterative cycles of hypothesis pre-registration, controlled experimental testing at scale, and theory falsification or refinement. The ultimate output is a suite of empirically validated, predictive scaling laws for capabilities, moving beyond descriptive explanation to actionable forecasting.

ideas.title: Distilling Emergent Generalism from a Collective of Efficient Specialists
ideas.description: This research challenges the paradigm that scale is necessary for general capability by investigating if a high-performing generalist model can be synthesized from a diverse ensemble of small, ultra-efficient specialist models. The core hypothesis is that the act of coordinating these specialists to solve complex, composite tasks forces the emergence of abstract, transferable representations and reasoning patterns within the ensemble's communication or aggregation layer. The inverse distillation process then aims to extract and crystallize these emergent structures into a single, small-scale generalist architecture, effectively 'reverse-engineering' general capability from efficiency and specialization rather than scaling.
